content.text:
In this environment you have access to a set of tools you can use to answer the user's question. 

You only have access to the tools provided below. You can only use one tool per message, and will receive the result of that tool in the user's next response. You use tools step-by-step to accomplish a given task, with each tool-use informed by the result of the previous tool-use. Today is: 2025-12-27

# Tool-Use Formatting Instructions 

Tool-use is formatted using XML-style tags. The tool-use is enclosed in <use_mcp_tool></use_mcp_tool> and each parameter is similarly enclosed within its own set of tags.

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) connects to servers that provide additional tools and resources to extend your capabilities. You can use the server's tools via the `use_mcp_tool`.

Description: 
Request to use a tool provided by a MCP server. Each MCP server can provide multiple tools with different capabilities. Tools have defined input schemas that specify required and optional parameters.

Parameters:
- server_name: (required) The name of the MCP server providing the tool
- tool_name: (required) The name of the tool to execute
- arguments: (required) A JSON object containing the tool's input parameters, following the tool's input schema, quotes within string must be properly escaped, ensure it's valid JSON

Usage:
<use_mcp_tool>
<server_name>server name here</server_name>
<tool_name>tool name here</tool_name>
<arguments>

{{
"param1": "value1",
"param2": "value2 \\"escaped string\\""
}}

</arguments>
</use_mcp_tool>

Important Notes:
- Tool-use must be placed **at the end** of your response, **top-level**, and not nested within other tags.
- Always adhere to this format for the tool use to ensure proper parsing and execution.

String and scalar parameters should be specified as is, while lists and objects should use JSON format. Note that spaces for string values are not stripped. The output is not expected to be valid XML and is parsed with regular expressions.
Here are the functions available in JSONSchema format:

## Server name: tool-searching
### Tool name: google_search
Description: Perform google searches via Serper API and retrieve rich results.
It is able to retrieve organic search results, people also ask, related searches, and knowledge graph.

Args:
    q: Search query string.
    gl: Country context for search (e.g., 'us' for United States, 'cn' for China, 'uk' for United Kingdom). Influences regional results priority. Default is 'us'.
    hl: Google interface language (e.g., 'en' for English, 'zh' for Chinese, 'es' for Spanish). Affects snippet language preference. Default is 'en'.
    location: City-level location for search results (e.g., 'SoHo, New York, United States', 'California, United States').
    num: The number of results to return (default: 10).
    tbs: Time-based search filter ('qdr:h' for past hour, 'qdr:d' for past day, 'qdr:w' for past week, 'qdr:m' for past month, 'qdr:y' for past year).
    page: The page number of results to return (default: 1).

Returns:
    The search results.
### Tool name: wiki_get_page_content
Description: Get specific Wikipedia page content for the specific entity (people, places, concepts, events) and return structured information.

This tool searches Wikipedia for the given entity and returns either the first few sentences
(which typically contain the summary/introduction) or full page content based on parameters.
It handles disambiguation pages and provides clean, structured output.

Args:
    entity: The entity to search for in Wikipedia.
    first_sentences: Number of first sentences to return from the page. Set to 0 to return full content. Defaults to 10.

Returns:
    str: Formatted search results containing title, first sentences/full content, and URL.
         Returns error message if page not found or other issues occur.
### Tool name: search_wiki_revision
Description: Search for an entity in Wikipedia and return the revision history for a specific month.

Args:
    entity: The entity to search for in Wikipedia.
    year: The year of the revision (e.g. 2024).
    month: The month of the revision (1-12).
    max_revisions: Maximum number of revisions to return. Defaults to 50.

Returns:
    str: Formatted revision history with timestamps, revision IDs, and URLs.
         Returns error message if page not found or other issues occur.
### Tool name: search_archived_webpage
Description: Search the Wayback Machine (archive.org) for archived versions of a webpage, optionally for a specific date.

Args:
    url: The URL to search for in the Wayback Machine.
    year: The target year (e.g., 2023).
    month: The target month (1-12).
    day: The target day (1-31).

Returns:
    str: Formatted archive information including archived URL, timestamp, and status.
         Returns error message if URL not found or other issues occur.
### Tool name: scrape_website
Description: This tool is used to scrape a website for its content. Search engines are not supported by this tool. This tool can also be used to get YouTube video non-visual information (however, it may be incomplete), such as video subtitles, titles, descriptions, key moments, etc.

Args:
    url: The URL of the website to scrape.
Returns:
    The scraped website content.
## Server name: tool-reasoning
### Tool name: reasoning
Description: This tool is for pure text-based reasoning, analysis, and logical thinking. It integrates collected information, organizes final logic, and provides planning insights.

IMPORTANT: This tool cannot access the internet, read files, program, or process multimodal content. It only performs pure text reasoning.

Use this tool for:
- Integrating and synthesizing collected information
- Analyzing patterns and relationships in data
- Logical reasoning and problem-solving
- Planning and strategy development
- Complex math problems, puzzles, riddles, and IQ tests

DO NOT use this tool for simple and obvious questions.

Args:
    question: The complex question or problem requiring step-by-step reasoning. Should include all relevant information needed to solve the problem.

Returns:
    The reasoned answer to the question.


# General Objective

You accomplish a given task iteratively, breaking it down into clear steps and working through them methodically.

## Task Strategy

1. Analyze the user's request and set clear, achievable sub-goals. Prioritize these sub-goals in a logical order.
2. Start with a concise, numbered, step-by-step plan (e.g., 1., 2., 3.) outlining how you will solve the task before taking any action. Each sub-goal should correspond to a distinct step in your task-solving process.
3. Work through these sub-goals sequentially. After each step, carefully review and extract all potentially relevant information, details, or implications from the tool result before proceeding. The user may provide tool-use feedback, reflect on the results, and revise your plan if needed. If you encounter new information or challenges, adjust your approach accordingly. Revisit previous steps to ensure earlier sub-goals or clues have not been overlooked or missed.
4. You have access to a wide range of powerful tools. Use them strategically to accomplish each sub-goal.

## Tool-Use Guidelines

1. **IMPORTANT: Each step must involve exactly ONE tool call only, unless the task is already solved. You are strictly prohibited from making multiple tool calls in a single response.** 
2. Before each tool call:
- Briefly summarize and analyze what is currently known.
- Identify what is missing, uncertain, or unreliable.
- Be concise; do not repeat the same analysis across steps.
- Choose the most relevant tool for the current sub-goal, and explain why this tool is necessary at this point.
- Verify whether all required parameters are either explicitly provided or can be clearly and reasonably inferred from context.
- Do not guess or use placeholder values for missing inputs.
- Skip optional parameters unless they are explicitly specified.
3. All tool queries must include full, self-contained context. Tools do not retain memory between calls. Include all relevant information from earlier steps in each query.
4. Avoid broad, vague, or speculative queries. Every tool call should aim to retrieve new, actionable information that clearly advances the task.
5. **For historical or time-specific content**: Regular search engines return current webpage content, not historical content. Archived webpage search is essential for retrieving content as it appeared in the past, use related tools to search for the historical content.
6. Even if a tool result does not directly answer the question, thoroughly extract and summarize all partial information, important details, patterns, constraints, or keywords that may help guide future steps. Never proceed to the next step without first ensuring that all significant insights from the current result have been fully considered.

## Tool-Use Communication Rules

1. **CRITICAL: After issuing exactly ONE tool call, STOP your response immediately. You must never make multiple tool calls in a single response. Do not include tool results, do not assume what the results will be, and do not continue with additional analysis or tool calls. The user will provide the actual tool results in their next message.**
2. Do not present the final answer until the entire task is complete.
3. Do not mention tool names.
4. Do not engage in unnecessary back-and-forth or end with vague offers of help. Do not end your responses with questions or generic prompts.
5. Do not use tools that do not exist.
6. Unless otherwise requested, respond in the same language as the user's message.
7. If the task does not require tool use, answer the user directly.

Is Spain oa country of Europe?

Your task is to comprehensively address the question by actively collecting detailed information from the web, and generating a thorough, transparent report. Your goal is NOT to rush a single definitive answer or conclusion, but rather to gather complete information and present ALL plausible candidate answers you find, accompanied by clearly documented supporting evidence, reasoning steps, uncertainties, and explicit intermediate findings.

User does not intend to set traps or create confusion on purpose. Handle the task using the most common, reasonable, and straightforward interpretation, and do not overthink or focus on rare or far-fetched interpretations.

Important considerations:
- Collect comprehensive information from reliable sources to understand all aspects of the question.
- Present every possible candidate answer identified during your information gathering, regardless of uncertainty, ambiguity, or incomplete verification. Avoid premature conclusions or omission of any discovered possibility.
- Explicitly document detailed facts, evidence, and reasoning steps supporting each candidate answer, carefully preserving intermediate analysis results.
- Clearly flag and retain any uncertainties, conflicting interpretations, or alternative understandings identified during information gathering. Do not arbitrarily discard or resolve these issues on your own.
- If the question's explicit instructions (e.g., numeric precision, formatting, specific requirements) appear inconsistent, unclear, erroneous, or potentially mismatched with general guidelines or provided examples, explicitly record and clearly present all plausible interpretations and corresponding candidate answers.  

Recognize that the original task description might itself contain mistakes, imprecision, inaccuracies, or conflicts introduced unintentionally by the user due to carelessness, misunderstanding, or limited expertise. Do NOT try to second-guess or "correct" these instructions internally; instead, transparently present findings according to every plausible interpretation.

Your objective is maximum completeness, transparency, and detailed documentation to empower the user to judge and select their preferred answer independently. Even if uncertain, explicitly documenting the existence of possible answers significantly enhances the user's experience, ensuring no plausible solution is irreversibly omitted due to early misunderstanding or premature filtering.

Before you begin, please review the following preliminary notes highlighting subtle or easily misunderstood points in the question, which might help you avoid common pitfalls during your analysis (for reference only; these may not be exhaustive):
Key Points & Potential Pitfalls to Watch For When Solving  

1. Typographical error  
   • The prompt reads “Is Spain oa country of Europe?” – clearly a typo for “a.”  
   • When searching online, use the corrected wording (“Is Spain a country of Europe?” / “Is Spain in Europe?”) to avoid irrelevant results triggered by the misspelling.

2. “Country of Europe” vs. “European Union”  
   • Many reputable sources quickly return facts about Spain’s membership in the European Union (EU).  
   • The question, however, asks whether Spain is a country located in (or belonging to) the continent of Europe, not whether it is an EU member. Keep the distinction clear when citing sources.

3. Continental vs. extra-continental territories  
   • Spain has territories outside mainland Europe (e.g., Canary Islands, Ceuta, Melilla).  
   • Don’t let these facts create confusion. Geographers still classify Spain as a European country because its principal landmass (Iberian Peninsula) lies in Europe.  
   • If a source seems to hedge on this point, verify whether it is discussing continental boundaries or political unions.

4. Possible ambiguity in phrasing (“of Europe”)  
   • “Of Europe” can be interpreted as “located in Europe” or “part of Europe’s political grouping.” Make sure to read sources in context so you cite geographic classification, not EU membership or Council of Europe, unless the source conflates them.

5. Reliability & authority of sources  
   • Because the factual question is simple, many quick-answer sites (Quora, Yahoo Answers, random blogs) appear high in search results. Prefer primary or highly authoritative references (Encyclopaedia Britannica, CIA World Factbook, official Spanish or EU portals, reputable atlases).

6. Up-to-date geopolitical references  
   • Although Spain’s status has not changed, double-check publication dates to avoid older texts that might use colonial or outdated descriptors which could confuse modern readers (especially pre-EU material that might casually conflate Europe with the EC/EEC).

7. Keep answer concise  
   • Avoid over-complicating. Provide a direct geographic confirmation, possibly noting the Iberian Peninsula location, and stop—no need for exhaustive history or EU details unless explicitly necessary.

8. Formatting considerations  
   • If a numerical fact such as area or population is mentioned for context, verify units (km² vs. mi²).  
   • For a yes/no type response, be aware that some platforms require a fully written sentence rather than a standalone “Yes.”

By keeping these points in mind, one can efficiently gather accurate information and present a clear, unambiguous answer.

Plan:
1) Run a targeted web search to gather authoritative sources that explicitly state Spain’s continental classification and geographic context, avoiding sources that conflate Europe with the EU.
2) From top credible sources (e.g., Encyclopaedia Britannica, CIA World Factbook, UN geoscheme, Wikipedia, National Geographic, official EU/Spanish portals), extract and document exact wordings that classify Spain as a European country, noting any mention of extra-European territories (e.g., Canary Islands) that might introduce nuance.
3) Compile a transparent report listing all candidate answers found (e.g., “Yes, Spain is a country in Europe”; “Mostly European but with non-European territories”), each with quoted evidence, links, dates, and any uncertainties or alternative interpretations.

What we know so far:
- Common knowledge suggests Spain is a country in southwestern Europe (Iberian Peninsula).
- Potential nuance: Spain possesses territories outside Europe (Canary Islands, Ceuta, Melilla).
What’s missing/uncertain:
- Explicit, up-to-date confirmations from multiple authoritative sources.
- Clear documentation of any sources that introduce ambiguity (e.g., “transcontinental” descriptions).
Why this tool now:
- A web search is needed to identify and collect multiple high-quality, citable sources for comprehensive evidence.

Proceeding to search for authoritative statements confirming Spain’s continental classification.

<use_mcp_tool>
<server_name>tool-searching</server_name>
<tool_name>google_search</tool_name>
<arguments>
{
  "q": "Is Spain in Europe? Spain country in southwestern Europe Britannica CIA World Factbook UN geoscheme",
  "gl": "us",
  "hl": "en",
  "num": 10
}
</arguments>
</use_mcp_tool>

Step-by-step plan:
1) Extract exact wording and citations from top authoritative sources confirming Spain’s continental classification (start with Encyclopaedia Britannica).
2) Gather corroborating statements from additional authoritative sources (CIA World Factbook, Wikipedia) including any nuance about extra-European territories.
3) If needed, add a standards-based regional classification source (e.g., UN M49/UN geoscheme) to show Spain’s placement in Europe/Southern Europe.
4) Compile a transparent report listing all candidate answers, with quotes, links, dates, and any uncertainties or alternative interpretations.

What we know so far:
- Initial search results show Britannica, CIA World Factbook, and Wikipedia indicating Spain is in (southwestern/southern) Europe.
What’s missing/uncertain:
- Exact wordings and page excerpts to quote as evidence.
- An explicit standards-based classification (e.g., UN geoscheme/M49).
Why this tool now:
- We need the precise Britannica text and publication context to cite accurately. Scraping the page provides authoritative wording.

Proceeding to retrieve Britannica’s page content for exact phrasing.

<use_mcp_tool>
<server_name>tool-searching</server_name>
<tool_name>scrape_website</tool_name>
<arguments>
{
  "url": "https://www.britannica.com/place/Spain"
}
</arguments>
</use_mcp_tool>

**Spain**, [country](https://www.britannica.com/topic/nation-state) located in extreme southwestern [Europe](https://www.britannica.com/place/Europe). It occupies about 85 percent of the [Iberian Peninsula](https://www.britannica.com/place/Iberian-Peninsula), which it shares with its smaller neighbor [Portugal](https://www.britannica.com/place/Portugal).

[![Image 34: Spain](https://cdn.britannica.com/61/183761-050-F02B165E/World-Data-Locator-Map-Spain.jpg?w=300) 1 of 2](https://cdn.britannica.com/61/183761-050-F02B165E/World-Data-Locator-Map-Spain.jpg)

[Spain](https://cdn.britannica.com/61/183761-050-F02B165E/World-Data-Locator-Map-Spain.jpg)(more)

[![Image 35: Andalusia, Spain](https://cdn.britannica.com/05/20305-050-C42D0918/village-Andalusia-region-Spain-housing.jpg?w=300) 2 of 2](https://cdn.britannica.com/05/20305-050-C42D0918/village-Andalusia-region-Spain-housing.jpg)

[Andalusia, Spain](https://cdn.britannica.com/05/20305-050-C42D0918/village-Andalusia-region-Spain-housing.jpg)This image of a village in Andalusia, Spain, shows housing typical of the region.(more)

Spain is a storied country of stone castles, snowcapped mountains, vast monuments, and sophisticated cities, all of which have made it a favored travel destination. The country is geographically and culturally [diverse](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/diverse). Its heartland is the [Meseta](https://www.britannica.com/place/Meseta-Central), a broad central [plateau](https://www.britannica.com/science/plateau-landform) half a mile above [sea level](https://www.britannica.com/science/sea-level). Much of the region is traditionally given over to [cattle](https://www.britannica.com/animal/cattle-livestock) ranching and [grain production](https://www.britannica.com/topic/cereal-farming); it was in this rural setting that [Miguel de Cervantes](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Miguel-de-Cervantes)’s [Don Quixote](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Don-Quixote-novel) tilted at the tall windmills that still dot the landscape in several places. In the country’s northeast are the broad valley of the [Ebro River](https://www.britannica.com/place/Ebro-River), the mountainous region of [Catalonia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Catalonia), and the hilly coastal plain of [Valencia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Valencia-province-Spain). To the northwest is the [Cantabrian Mountains](https://www.britannica.com/place/Cantabrian-Mountains), a rugged range in which heavily forested, rain-swept valleys are interspersed with tall peaks. To the south is the citrus-orchard-rich and irrigated lands of the valley of the [Guadalquivir River](https://www.britannica.com/place/Guadalquivir-River), celebrated in the renowned lyrics of Spanish poets [Federico García Lorca](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Federico-Garcia-Lorca) and [Antonio Machado](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Antonio-Machado); over this valley rises the snowcapped [Sierra Nevada](https://www.britannica.com/place/Sierra-Nevada-mountain-range-Spain). The southern portion of the country is desert, an extension of the Sahara made familiar to Americans through the “spaghetti western” films of the 1960s and early ’70s. Lined with palm trees, rosemary bushes, and other tropical vegetation, the southeastern Mediterranean coast and the [Balearic Islands](https://www.britannica.com/place/Balearic-Islands) enjoy a gentle climate, drawing millions of visitors and retirees, especially from northern Europe.

Spain’s countryside is quaint, speckled with castles, aqueducts, and ancient ruins, but its cities are resoundingly modern. The Andalusian capital of [Sevilla](https://www.britannica.com/place/Sevilla-Spain) (Seville) is famed for its musical [culture](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/culture) and traditional folkways; the Catalonian capital of [Barcelona](https://www.britannica.com/place/Barcelona) for its [secular](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/secular) architecture and maritime industry; and the national capital of [Madrid](https://www.britannica.com/place/Madrid) for its winding streets, its museums and bookstores, and its around-the-clock lifestyle. Madrid is Spain’s largest city and is also its financial and cultural center, as it has been for hundreds of years.

The many and varied [cultures](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/cultures) that have gone into the making of Spain—those of the Castilians, Catalonians, Lusitanians, Galicians, Basques, Romans, Arabs, Jews, and Roma (Gypsies), among other peoples—are renowned for their varied cuisines, customs, and [prolific](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/prolific) contributions to the world’s artistic heritage. The country’s Roman conquerors left their language, roads, and monuments, while many of the Roman Empire’s greatest rulers were Spanish, among them [Trajan](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Trajan), [Hadrian](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hadrian), and [Marcus Aurelius](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Marcus-Aurelius-Roman-emperor). The Moors, who ruled over portions of Spain for nearly 800 years, left a [legacy](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/legacy) of fine architecture, [lyric poetry](https://www.britannica.com/art/lyric), and science; the Roma contributed the haunting music called the [_cante jondo_](https://www.britannica.com/art/cante-jondo) (a form of [flamenco](https://www.britannica.com/art/flamenco)), which, wrote García [Lorca](https://www.britannica.com/place/Lorca), “comes from remote races and crosses the graveyard of the years and the fronds of parched winds. It comes from the first sob and the first kiss.” Even the Vandals, Huns, and Visigoths who swept across Spain following the fall of Rome are remembered in words and monuments, which prompted García Lorca to remark, “In Spain, the dead are more alive than the dead of any other country in the world.”

[![Image 40: Christopher Columbus's fleet](https://cdn.britannica.com/12/94912-050-01938555/Illustration-Christopher-Columbus-fleet-Spain-1492.jpg?w=300)](https://cdn.britannica.com/12/94912-050-01938555/Illustration-Christopher-Columbus-fleet-Spain-1492.jpg)

[Christopher Columbus's fleet](https://cdn.britannica.com/12/94912-050-01938555/Illustration-Christopher-Columbus-fleet-Spain-1492.jpg)Illustration depicting Christopher Columbus's fleet departing from Spain in 1492.(more)

In 1492, the year the last of the [Moorish](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Moor-people) rulers were [expelled from Spain](https://www.britannica.com/event/Reconquista), ships under the command of [Christopher Columbus](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Christopher-Columbus) reached [America](https://www.britannica.com/place/United-States). For 300 years afterward, Spanish explorers and conquerors traveled the world, claiming huge territories for the Spanish [crown](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/crown), a succession of Castilian, Aragonese, [Habsburg](https://www.britannica.com/topic/House-of-Habsburg), and [Bourbon](https://www.britannica.com/topic/house-of-Bourbon) rulers. For generations Spain was arguably the richest country in the world, and certainly the most far-flung. With the steady erosion of its continental and overseas empire throughout the 18th and 19th centuries, however, Spain was all but forgotten in world affairs, save for the three years that the ideologically charged [Spanish Civil War](https://www.britannica.com/event/Spanish-Civil-War) (1936–39) put the country at the center of the world’s stage, only to become ever more insular and withdrawn during the four decades of rule by dictator [Francisco Franco](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Francisco-Franco). Following Franco’s death in 1975, a Bourbon king, [Juan Carlos](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Juan-Carlos), returned to the throne and established a [constitutional monarchy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/constitutional-monarchy). The country has been ruled since then by a succession of elected governments, some socialist, some [conservative](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/conservative), but all devoted to [democracy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/democracy).

[![Image 41: Flags of all nations of the world. Grouping of various country flags on a world map.](https://cdn.britannica.com/64/244164-131-C5EE8DAB/nation-flags-world-map.jpg) Britannica Quiz Which Country Is Larger? Quiz](https://www.britannica.com/quiz/which-country-is-larger-quiz)
Land
----

[![Image 42: Ibiza](https://cdn.britannica.com/36/10836-050-E2186916/Ibiza-city-port-Spain.jpg?w=300)](https://cdn.britannica.com/36/10836-050-E2186916/Ibiza-city-port-Spain.jpg)

[Ibiza](https://cdn.britannica.com/36/10836-050-E2186916/Ibiza-city-port-Spain.jpg)Ibiza city and port, Spain.(more)

Spain is bordered to the west by Portugal; to the northeast it borders [France](https://www.britannica.com/place/France), from which it is separated by the tiny principality of [Andorra](https://www.britannica.com/place/Andorra) and by the great wall of the [Pyrenees](https://www.britannica.com/place/Pyrenees) Mountains. Spain’s only other land border is in the far south with [Gibraltar](https://www.britannica.com/place/Gibraltar), an enclave that belonged to Spain until 1713, when it was ceded to Great Britain in the Treaty of Utrecht at the end of the [War of the Spanish Succession](https://www.britannica.com/event/War-of-the-Spanish-Succession). Elsewhere the country is bounded by water: by the [Mediterranean Sea](https://www.britannica.com/place/Mediterranean-Sea) to the east and southeast, by the [Atlantic Ocean](https://www.britannica.com/place/Atlantic-Ocean) to the northwest and southwest, and by the [Bay of Biscay](https://www.britannica.com/place/Bay-of-Biscay) (an inlet of the Atlantic Ocean) to the north. The [Canary (Canarias) Islands](https://www.britannica.com/place/Canary-Islands), in the Atlantic Ocean off the northwestern African mainland, and the [Balearic (Baleares) Islands](https://www.britannica.com/place/Balearic-Islands), in the Mediterranean, also are parts of Spain, as are [Ceuta](https://www.britannica.com/place/Ceuta) and [Melilla](https://www.britannica.com/place/Melilla), two small enclaves in [North Africa](https://www.britannica.com/place/North-Africa) (northern Morocco) that Spain has ruled for centuries.

[Iberian Peninsula; International Space Station](https://cdn.britannica.com/49/194349-050-6C1FBC57/Iberian-Peninsula-International-Space-Station-July-26-2014.jpg)The Iberian Peninsula seen from the International Space Station, July 26, 2014.(more)

Spain accounts for five-sixths of the Iberian Peninsula, the roughly quadrilateral southwestern tip of Europe that separates the Mediterranean Sea from the Atlantic Ocean. Most of Spain [comprises](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/comprises) a large plateau (the [Meseta Central](https://www.britannica.com/place/Meseta-Central)) divided by a mountain range, the Central Sierra (Sistema Central), which trends west-southwest to east-northeast. Several mountains border the plateau: the [Cantabrian Mountains](https://www.britannica.com/place/Cantabrian-Mountains) (Cordillera Cantábrica) to the north, the Iberian Cordillera (Sistema Ibérico) to the northeast and east, the [Sierra Morena](https://www.britannica.com/place/Sierra-Morena) to the south, and the lower mountains of the Portuguese frontier and Spanish Galicia to the northwest. The Pyrenees run across the neck of the peninsula and form Spain’s border with France. There are two major depressions, that of the [Ebro](https://www.britannica.com/place/Ebro-River) River in the northeast and that of the [Guadalquivir River](https://www.britannica.com/place/Guadalquivir-River) in the southwest. In the southeast the [Baetic Cordillera](https://www.britannica.com/place/Baetic-Cordillera) (Sistema Penibético) runs broadly parallel to the coast to merge with the mountains of the Iberian Cordillera. Along the Mediterranean seaboard there are coastal plains, some with lagoons (e.g., Albufera, south of [Valencia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Valencia-Spain)). Offshore in the Mediterranean, the Balearic Islands are an unsubmerged portion of the Baetic Cordillera. The [Canary Islands](https://www.britannica.com/place/Canary-Islands) in the Atlantic are of volcanic origin and contain the highest peak on Spanish territory, [Teide Peak](https://www.britannica.com/place/Teide-Peak), which rises to 12,198 feet (3,718 meters) on the island of [Tenerife](https://www.britannica.com/place/Tenerife).

Spain has some of the oldest as well as some of the youngest rocks of Europe. The entire western half of [Iberia](https://www.britannica.com/money/Iberia-Spanish-airline), with the exception of the extreme south, is composed of ancient ([Hercynian](https://www.britannica.com/place/Variscan-orogenic-belt)) rocks; geologists refer to this Hercynian block as the [Meseta Central](https://www.britannica.com/place/Meseta-Central). It [constitutes](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/constitutes) a relatively stable platform around which younger sediments accumulated, especially on the Mediterranean side. In due course these sediments were pushed by major earth movements into mountain ranges. The term _meseta_ is also used by geographers and local toponymy to designate the dominating relief unit of central Iberia. As a result, the Meseta Central defined by relief is subdivided by geology into a crystalline west (granites and gneisses) and a sedimentary east (mainly clays and limestones). The northern Meseta Central, which has an average elevation of 2,300 feet (700 meters), corresponds to the tablelands, or plateau, of [Castile and León](https://www.britannica.com/place/Castile-Leon), although it is in fact a [basin](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/basin) surrounded by mountains and drained by the Douro (Duero) River. The southern Meseta Central (the Meseta of Castile–La Mancha) is some 330 feet (100 meters) lower. Its relief is more diverse, however, owing to heavy faulting and warping caused by [volcanic activity](https://www.britannica.com/science/volcanism) around the Calatrava Plain and to two complex river systems (the Guadiana and the Tagus) separated by mountains. Its southern plains rise gradually to the Sierra Morena. The southeastern side of this range drops almost vertically by more than 3,300 feet (1,000 meters) to the Guadalquivir depression. Dividing the northern and southern Mesetas are the Central Sierras, one of the outstanding features of the Iberian massif. Their highest points—Peñalara Peak at 7,972 feet (2,430 meters) and Almanzor Peak at 8,497 feet (2,590 meters)—rise well above the plains of the central [plateau](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/plateau). In contrast, the granitic Galician mountains, at the northwestern end of the Hercynian block, have an average elevation of only 1,640 feet (500 meters), decreasing toward the deeply indented ([ria](https://www.britannica.com/science/ria)) coast of the Atlantic seaboard.

Part of Alpine Europe, the [Pyrenees](https://www.britannica.com/place/Pyrenees) form a massive mountain range that stretches from the Mediterranean Sea to the Bay of Biscay, a distance of some 270 miles (430 km). The range comprises a series of parallel zones: the central axis, a line of intermediate depressions, and the pre-Pyrenees. The highest peaks, formed from a core of ancient crystalline rocks, are found in the central Pyrenees—notably [Aneto Peak](https://www.britannica.com/place/Pico-de-Aneto) at 11,168 feet (3,404 meters)—but those of the west, including [Anie Peak](https://www.britannica.com/place/Anie-Peak) at 8,213 feet (2,503 meters), are not much lower. The mountains fall steeply on the northern side but [descend](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/descend) in terraces to the Ebro River trough in the south. The outer zones of the Pyrenees are composed of sedimentary rocks. Relief on the nearly horizontal sedimentary strata of the Ebro depression is mostly plain or plateau, except at the eastern end where the Ebro River penetrates the mountains to reach the Mediterranean Sea.

A series of sierras trending northwest-southeast forms the Iberian Cordillera, which separates the Ebro depression from the Meseta and reaches its highest elevation with Moncayo Peak at 7,588 feet (2,313 meters). In the southeast the Iberian Cordillera links with the [Baetic Cordillera](https://www.britannica.com/place/Baetic-Cordillera), also a result of Alpine earth movements. Although more extensive—more than 500 miles (800 km) long and up to 150 miles (240 km) wide—and with [peninsular](https://www.britannica.com/topic/peninsular) Spain’s highest [summit](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/summit), [Mulhacén Peak](https://www.britannica.com/place/Mount-Mulhacen), at 11,421 feet (3,481 meters), the Baetic ranges are more fragmented and less of a barrier than the Pyrenees. On their northern and northwestern sides they flank the low-lying and fairly flat Guadalquivir basin, the average elevation of which is only 426 feet (130 meters) on mainly clay strata. Unlike the Ebro basin, the Guadalquivir depression is wide open to the sea on the southwest, and its delta has extensive marshland ([Las Marismas](https://www.britannica.com/place/Las-Marismas)).

Drainage
--------

[![Image 47: Júcar River and a 14th-century castle](https://cdn.britannica.com/89/10589-050-39E7A445/Jucar-River-castle-Valencia-Spain-Cofrentes.jpg?w=300)](https://cdn.britannica.com/89/10589-050-39E7A445/Jucar-River-castle-Valencia-Spain-Cofrentes.jpg)

[Júcar River and a 14th-century castle](https://cdn.britannica.com/89/10589-050-39E7A445/Jucar-River-castle-Valencia-Spain-Cofrentes.jpg)The Júcar River flowing past a 14th-century castle at Cofrentes, Valencia, Spain.(more)

Although some maintain that “aridity rivals civil war as the chief curse of [historic] Spain,” the Iberian Peninsula has a dense network of streams, three of which rank among Europe’s longest: the [Tagus](https://www.britannica.com/place/Tagus-River) at 626 miles (1,007 km), the [Ebro](https://www.britannica.com/place/Ebro-River) at 565 miles (909 km), and the [Douro](https://www.britannica.com/place/Douro-River) at 556 miles (895 km). The [Guadiana](https://www.britannica.com/place/Guadiana-River) and the [Guadalquivir](https://www.britannica.com/place/Guadalquivir-River) are 508 miles (818 km) and 408 miles (657 km) long, respectively. The Tagus, like the Douro and the Guadiana, reaches the Atlantic Ocean in Portugal. In fact, all the major rivers of Spain except the Ebro drain into the Atlantic Ocean. The hydrographic network on the Mediterranean side of the [watershed](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/watershed) is poorly developed in comparison with the Atlantic systems, partly because it falls into the climatically driest parts of Spain. However, nearly all Iberian rivers have low annual volume, irregular regimes, and deep valleys and even canyons. Flooding is always a potential hazard. The short, swift streams of [Galicia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Galicia-region-Spain) and [Cantabria](https://www.britannica.com/place/Cantabria-province-Spain), draining to the northwestern and northern coasts, respectively, have only a slight or, at most, modest summer minimum. The predominant fluvial regime in Spain is thus characterized by a long or very long summer period of low water. This is the regime of all the major arteries that drain the Meseta as well as those of the Mediterranean seaboard, such as the [Júcar](https://www.britannica.com/place/Jucar-River) and the [Segura](https://www.britannica.com/place/Segura-River): for example, from August to September the Guadiana River usually has less than one-tenth of its average annual flow. Only the Ebro River has a relatively constant and substantial flow—19,081 cubic feet (540 cubic meters) per second at Tortosa—coming from snowmelt as well as rainfall in the high Pyrenees. In comparison, the flow of the Douro is only 5,050 cubic feet (143 cubic meters) per second. The flow of many Iberian streams has been reduced artificially by water extraction for purposes such as irrigation. Subterranean flow is well-developed in limestone districts.

Soils
-----

There are five major soil types in Spain. Two are widely distributed but of limited extent: alluvial soils, found in the major valleys and coastal plains, and poorly developed, or [truncated](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/truncated), mountain soils. Brown forest soils are restricted to humid Galicia and [Cantabria](https://www.britannica.com/place/Cantabria-autonomous-area-and-region-Spain). Acidic southern brown earths (leading to restricted crop choice) are prevalent on the crystalline rocks of the western Meseta, and gray, brown, or chestnut soils have developed on the calcareous and alkaline strata of the eastern Meseta and of eastern Spain in general. Saline soils are found in the Ebro basin and coastal lowlands. Calcretes (subsoil zonal crusts [toscas], usually of hardened calcium carbonate) are particularly well-developed in the arid regions of the east: [La Mancha](https://www.britannica.com/place/La-Mancha), [Almería](https://www.britannica.com/place/Almeria-province-Spain), [Murcia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Murcia-region-Spain), [Alicante](https://www.britannica.com/place/Alicante-province-Spain) (Alacant), and [Valencia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Valencia-province-Spain), as well as the Ebro and [Lleida](https://www.britannica.com/place/Lleida-province-Spain) (Lérida) basins.

Soil erosion resulting from the vegetation [degradation](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/degradation) suffered by Spain for at least the past 3,000 years has created extensive badlands, reduced soil cover, downstream alluviation, and, more recently, silting of dams and irrigation works. Particularly affected are the high areas of the central plateau and southern and eastern parts of Spain. Although the origins of some of the spectacular badlands of southeastern Spain, such as [Guadix](https://www.britannica.com/place/Guadix), may lie in climatic conditions from earlier in Quaternary time (beginning 2.6 million years ago), one of the major problems of modern Spain is the threat of desertification—i.e., the impoverishment of arid, semiarid, and even some humid ecosystems caused by the joint impact of human activities and [drought](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/drought). Nearly half of Spain is moderately or severely affected, especially in the arid east (Almería, Murcia), as well as in much of subarid Spain (the Ebro basin). The government has adopted policies of afforestation, but some authorities believe that natural vegetation regrowth would yield more speedy and more permanent benefits.

Climate of Spain
================

Spain is characterized by the overlap of one fundamental climatic division (between humid and semiarid and arid zones) by another (the threefold division of the peninsula into maritime, continental, and mountain climates). This complexity results from the peninsula’s size, which is large enough to generate a continental thermal regime; its location close to the [Atlantic Ocean](https://www.britannica.com/place/Atlantic-Ocean) and [North Africa](https://www.britannica.com/place/North-Africa), exposing it to both [maritime](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/maritime) and Saharan influences; and its mountainous relief, which not only produces its own climatic zones but also exaggerates local aridity through the creation of rain shadows on the mountains’ leeward sides.

The [Pyrenees](https://www.britannica.com/place/Pyrenees) and the Cantabrian ranges play an important role in the Spanish climate, holding the warm, dry subtropical airstream over Spain during the summer months. In general, westerly winds from the North Atlantic are dominant most of the year, while the warm, dry Saharan airstream blows less frequently. Some local or seasonal winds are notable: the easterly _levante_ ([levanter](https://www.britannica.com/science/levanter)) can bring as many as 15 consecutive days of dry, clear weather to the coastal strip in the region of the Strait of Gibraltar; the _leveche_ brings a hot, dry, dust-laden wind that [blights](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/blights) vegetation in spring from the southern sector to the Spanish Levantine lowlands (the provinces of [Castellón](https://www.britannica.com/place/Castellon), [Valencia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Valencia-autonomous-area-Spain), and Alicante); and in spring and summer a wind from the same sector, the _solano_, carries unbearably hot, dry, suffocating weather over the Andalusian plain. Northern Spain, from [Galicia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Galicia-region-Spain) to northern [Catalonia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Catalonia) (Catalunya, or Cataluña), is characterized by a temperate humid or maritime type of climate, having high rainfall and an average temperature in January of 43 °F (6 °C) near the coast but less than that inland and in the mountains. [A Coruña](https://www.britannica.com/place/A-Coruna-Spain) (La Coruña) has a moderate annual temperature, ranging from 48 °F (9 °C) in winter to 64 °F (18 °C) in summer, and the annual rainfall is about 38 inches (965 mm). The rest of the peninsula has a Mediterranean type of climate with continental tendencies—i.e., hot toward the coast, relatively cold in the interior, humid only in the mountains, and dry elsewhere. Thus [Albacete](https://www.britannica.com/place/Albacete-Spain), in the southeastern part of the southern Meseta, varies between 40 °F (4 °C) in the winter and 75 °F (24 °C) in the summer, while the annual rainfall is less than 15 inches (380 mm). The valleys of the Ebro and the Guadalquivir also have a continental climate, the Ebro drier and colder and the Guadalquivir warmer and more humid. Catalonia, Valencia, and the [Balearic Islands](https://www.britannica.com/place/Balearic-Islands) enjoy more temperate weather, with higher rainfall in Catalonia, while the [Canary Islands](https://www.britannica.com/place/Canary-Islands) have a subtropical Atlantic climate.

Plant and animal life
---------------------

Vegetation
----------

Nearly half of Spain is covered by spontaneous vegetation of some sort, but only a small proportion (largely confined to the mountains) is classified as dense woodland. Northern Spain has heath and deciduous woodland (oak, beech). The mountains of the northern Meseta and the Iberian and Baetic cordilleras carry deciduous Portuguese oak; those of the central Pyrenees, the Iberian ranges, and the Central Sierras have [diverse](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/diverse) pine species. The rest, more than half of Spain, has a [Mediterranean vegetation](https://www.britannica.com/plant/Mediterranean-vegetation) characterized by [evergreen oak](https://www.britannica.com/plant/evergreen-oak) (_Quercus ilex_) and other drought-resistant plants commonly reduced to scrub status (_matorral_). An [esparto](https://www.britannica.com/plant/esparto) grass (_[Lygeum spartum](https://www.britannica.com/plant/Lygeum-spartum)_) is found in the steppes of [La Mancha](https://www.britannica.com/place/La-Mancha) and the southeast; the esparto products of Spain (paper, rope, basketry), however, come from an associated alfa grass (_[Stipa tenacissima](https://www.britannica.com/plant/Stipa-tenacissima)_). Poplar and eucalyptus have become widespread since the 19th century.

Wildlife
--------

The proximity of Africa has given Spain more African species of wildlife than are found in the other Mediterranean peninsulas, while the Pyrenean barrier and the general extent of the [country](https://www.britannica.com/topic/nation-state) explain the number of [indigenous](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/indigenous) species. The European wolf and the [brown bear](https://www.britannica.com/animal/brown-bear) survive in the scarce wild areas of the northeast. The [Barbary ape](https://www.britannica.com/animal/Barbary-macaque) is possibly indigenous but is more likely an import from North Africa. It survives only under protection, at [Gibraltar](https://www.britannica.com/place/Gibraltar). The wild boar, ibex (wild goat), and red and [fallow deer](https://www.britannica.com/animal/fallow-deer) are more common. More than half of the bird species of [Europe](https://www.britannica.com/place/Europe) are found in [Coto Doñana National Park](https://www.britannica.com/place/Coto-Donana-National-Park), at the mouth of the Guadalquivir; the Spanish imperial eagle and other large species such as the [eagle owl](https://www.britannica.com/animal/eagle-owl), the buzzard, and several varieties of pheasant are native to the high Pyrenees. Desert locusts have been known to invade southern Spain from North Africa.

Fish
----

The country’s waters contain a [diversity](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/diversity) of fish and shellfish, especially in the southeast where Atlantic and Mediterranean waters mix (the Alborán Sea). Species include red mullet, mackerel, tuna, octopus, swordfish, pilchard (_Sardinia pilchardus_), and [anchovy](https://www.britannica.com/animal/anchovy) (_Engraulis encrasicholus_). Demersal (bottom-dwelling) species include hake and whiting. Striped dolphin and the long-finned whale inhabit the waters off southeastern Spain, and the [bottlenose dolphin](https://www.britannica.com/animal/bottlenose-dolphin) is found off the Ebro delta. Overfishing has tended to alter the balance of species.

[Catherine Delano Smith](https://www.britannica.com/contributor/Catherine-Delano-Smith/3926)[Vicente Rodriguez](https://www.britannica.com/contributor/Vicente-Rodriguez/5003)
People
------

Ethnic groups
-------------

Spain has been invaded and inhabited by many different peoples. The peninsula was originally settled by groups from North Africa and western Europe, including the Iberians, Celts, and Basques. Throughout antiquity it was a constant point of attraction for the civilizations of the eastern Mediterranean. From _c._ 1100 bce the Phoenicians, the Greeks, and the Carthaginians began to establish settlements and trading posts, especially on the eastern and southern coasts. These outsiders found a mosaic of peoples, collectively known as the [Iberians](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Iberian), who did not have a single [culture](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/culture) or even share a single language. A kingdom called [Tartessus](https://www.britannica.com/place/Tartessus), which flourished between 800 and 550 bce, ruled much of the valley of the Guadalquivir. Elsewhere political organization was less sophisticated, consisting of a number of city-states in the coastal regions and of clans in the interior and the northwest.

The [Romans](https://www.britannica.com/place/ancient-Rome)
-----------------------------------------------------------

The Phoenician and Greek presence was limited to small coastal regions. The Carthaginians were the first to move inland; late in the 3rd century bce they set out to conquer as much of the peninsula as they could. [Yet](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/Yet) their success led to intervention in [Iberia](https://www.britannica.com/money/Iberia-Spanish-airline) from the Romans, who quickly drove out the Carthaginians and conquered much of the peninsula. The Romans, however, had to deal with a number of revolts, and it was only in 19 bce, after almost 200 years of warfare, that they secured their rule over all of Iberia. The Romans brought Iberia under a single political authority for the first time but did not try to impose a single culture on the inhabitants. Nevertheless, much of the indigenous elite adopted Roman culture and became Roman citizens, particularly in the south and east, where the Roman presence was strongest.

The [Visigoths](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Visigoth)
----------------------------------------------------------

Roman power in Spain collapsed during the 5th century ce when a number of Germanic peoples—the [Suebi](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Suebi), the [Alani](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Alani), the [Vandals](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Vandal-Germanic-people), and finally the [Visigoths](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Visigoth)—invaded the peninsula. At the end of the 6th century, King [Leovigild](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Leovigild) brought all of Spain under Visigothic rule, and his son Reccared [imposed](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/imposed) a single religion, Catholic [Christianity](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Christianity), on the country.

The [Muslims](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Islamic-world)
-------------------------------------------------------------

Visigothic rule did not last long. In 711 Muslim Arabs invaded Spain from North Africa and defeated the Visigothic ruler, King [Roderick](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Roderick). They quickly conquered almost the entire peninsula and established Muslim states in Spain that were to last until 1492.

Recent arrivals
---------------

The Muslims were the last new peoples to arrive in Spain in large numbers for many centuries. Indeed, from the 16th century on and especially during the 100 years after 1860, Spain was a country of emigration rather than immigration. This began to change in the 1980s when Spain’s new position as a highly industrialized and relatively prosperous country made it attractive to people from the developing world. For the first time since the Middle Ages, Spain received large numbers of immigrants. By the early 21st century there were several million legal foreign residents and illegal immigrants in Spain, the latter concentrated mainly in [Andalusia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Andalusia-region-Spain) (Andalucía), in metropolitan Madrid and [Barcelona](https://www.britannica.com/place/Barcelona-province-Spain), and in the Balearic and Canary islands. Most foreign residents came from other countries of the [European Union](https://www.britannica.com/topic/European-Union) (EU) and from Latin America. Many also arrived from [Morocco](https://www.britannica.com/place/Morocco), often crossing the [Strait of Gibraltar](https://www.britannica.com/place/Strait-of-Gibraltar) in small boats, and from [sub-Saharan Africa](https://www.britannica.com/place/sub-Saharan-Africa), arriving often at the Canary Islands; there also are significant numbers of Asians and Europeans from non-EU countries. Since 1985 Spanish governments have passed several laws on foreigners, which have made it more difficult for people to enter Spain and easier for the authorities to deport them. [Promulgated](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Promulgated) in 2000 (and subsequently modified), the Law on the Rights and Freedoms of Foreigners in Spain and Their Social [Integration](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Integration) sought to end the restrictive policies of the previous 15 years, terminating the practice of repatriating illegal immigrants and giving legal status to any employed illegal immigrant who resided in Spain for at least two years. In 2005 legislation legalized the status of many immigrant workers. The law also gave immigrants most of the same rights as Spanish citizens (except the right to vote).

The [Gitano](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Gitano) minority
--------------------------------------------------------------

The one ethnic minority of long standing in Spain is the [Roma](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Rom) (Gypsies), who are known in Spain as [Gitanos](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Rom). Their traditional language is Caló. Many of them have [assimilated](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/assimilated) into the mainstream of Spanish society, but others continue to lead their traditional nomadic way of life. The Gitanos were at one time most numerous in southern Spain, and, while there continue to be large populations in Andalusian cities such as [Almería](https://www.britannica.com/place/Almeria-province-Spain), [Granada](https://www.britannica.com/place/Granada-province-Spain), and [Murcia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Murcia-Spanish-kingdom), large [communities](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/communities) now exist in Madrid and Barcelona as well. [Flamenco](https://www.britannica.com/art/flamenco), an expressive song-dance form, has long been associated with the Gitanos.

Considerable [prejudice](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/prejudice) and [discrimination](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/discrimination) have existed against the Gitanos in Spain and are still prevalent today. But Gitanos have begun to create their own political organizations, such as the Union of the Gitano People (Unión del Pueblo Gitano; also known as the Unión Romaní), and some have been elected to parliament. There also are government programs that promote Gitano culture.

Languages
---------

The official language of Spain is [Castilian](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Castilian-dialect). It is the country’s most widely [spoken language](https://www.britannica.com/topic/speech-language), and outside Spain it is generally known as [Spanish](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Spanish-language). The constitution of Spain allows for its [autonomous](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/autonomous) communities to recognize their dominant regional languages and [dialects](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/dialects) as having official status along with Castilian. The statutes of 6 of the 17 autonomous communities [stipulate](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/stipulate) the following “co-official” languages: [Catalan](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Catalan-language) in Catalonia and in the Balearic Islands, Valencian in Valencia, Galician (Gallego) in Galicia, and Euskera ([Basque](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Basque-language)) in the [Basque Country](https://www.britannica.com/place/Basque-Country-region-Spain) and in some Euskera-speaking territories of [Navarra](https://www.britannica.com/place/Navarra). Although not named a co-official language of [Asturias](https://www.britannica.com/place/Asturias-region-Spain), Bable (Asturian) is protected and promoted under the community’s statutes, as are local Aragonese dialects in [Aragon](https://www.britannica.com/place/Aragon-region-Spain). In addition, Aranese, spoken in the Aran Valley, is safeguarded in a provision by the region’s government, the [autonomy](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/autonomy) of Catalonia. All of these languages except Euskera are [Romance languages](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Romance-languages) (i.e., they evolved from Latin). With no relation to any other language of the world, Euskera is what is known as a language isolate. Within their respective regions of dominance, many of the languages of Spain are taught regularly in school and are used in newspapers and radio and television broadcasts.

Step-by-step plan:
1) Capture exact, citable wording from a second authoritative source that explicitly classifies Spain’s continental location and notes any extra-continental territories, to complement Britannica.
2) Next, gather corroboration from the CIA World Factbook and a standards-based regional classification (UN M49/geoscheme).
3) Compile a transparent report listing all candidate answers, with quotes, links, dates, and any uncertainties or alternative interpretations.

What we know so far:
- Britannica explicitly states: “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe,” and mentions the Canary Islands, Ceuta, and Melilla.
What’s missing/uncertain:
- A second high-credibility source’s exact wording (e.g., Wikipedia) that also highlights transcontinental nuance (territories in North Africa).
Why this tool now:
- Scraping Wikipedia’s Spain page will provide the precise lead sentence(s) and any mention of territories outside Europe, which is directly relevant to potential nuances in interpreting “of Europe.”

Proceeding to retrieve Wikipedia’s Spain page for exact phrasing.

**Spain**,[[g]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-22) officially the **Kingdom of Spain**,[[h]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-nation_name-23) is a country in [Southern](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe") and [Western Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") with territories in [North Africa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa").[[i]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-24) Featuring the southernmost point of [continental Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"), it is the largest country in Southern Europe and the fourth-most populous [European Union](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU) member state. Spanning the majority of the [Iberian Peninsula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula"), its territory also includes the [Canary Islands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands"), in the Eastern [Atlantic Ocean](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"); the [Balearic Islands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balearic_Islands "Balearic Islands"), in the Western [Mediterranean Sea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"); and the autonomous cities of [Ceuta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ceuta "Ceuta") and [Melilla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melilla "Melilla"), in mainland Africa. Peninsular Spain is bordered to the north by [France](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/France "France"), [Andorra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andorra "Andorra"), and the [Bay of Biscay](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bay_of_Biscay "Bay of Biscay"); to the east and south by the Mediterranean Sea and [Gibraltar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar"); and to the west by [Portugal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") and the Atlantic Ocean. Spain's capital and [largest city](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_largest_cities_in_Spain "List of largest cities in Spain") is [Madrid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"); other major [urban areas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_in_Spain "List of metropolitan areas in Spain") include [Barcelona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), [Valencia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Valencia "Valencia"), [Seville](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seville "Seville"), [Zaragoza](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zaragoza "Zaragoza"), [Málaga](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M%C3%A1laga "Málaga"), [Murcia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Murcia "Murcia"), and [Palma de Mallorca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palma_de_Mallorca "Palma de Mallorca").

In early antiquity, the Iberian Peninsula was inhabited by [Celts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Celts "Celts"), [Iberians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iberians "Iberians"), and other [pre-Roman peoples](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_the_Pre-Roman_peoples_of_the_Iberian_Peninsula "List of the Pre-Roman peoples of the Iberian Peninsula"). The [Roman conquest of the Iberian peninsula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_the_Iberian_peninsula "Roman conquest of the Iberian peninsula") created the province of [Hispania](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hispania "Hispania"), which became deeply [Romanised](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Romanization_(cultural) "Romanization (cultural)") and later [Christianised](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christianization "Christianization"). After the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire"), the peninsula was conquered by tribes from Central Europe, among them the [Visigoths](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths"), who established the [Visigothic Kingdom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visigothic_Kingdom "Visigothic Kingdom") centred on [Toledo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toledo,_Spain "Toledo, Spain"). In the early 8th century, most of the peninsula was [conquered by the Umayyad Caliphate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Umayyad_conquest_of_Hispania "Umayyad conquest of Hispania"), with [Al-Andalus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus") centred on [Córdoba](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Spain "Córdoba, Spain"). The northern Christian kingdoms of Iberia launched the so-called _[Reconquista](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista")_, gradually repelling and ultimately expelling Islamic rule from the peninsula, culminating with the fall of the [Nasrid Kingdom of Granada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emirate_of_Granada "Emirate of Granada"). The dynastic union of the [Crown of Castile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile") and the [Crown of Aragon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon") in 1479 under the [Catholic Monarchs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catholic_Monarchs "Catholic Monarchs") is often seen as the _de facto_ unification of Spain as a [nation state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state").

During the [Age of Discovery](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"), Spain led the [exploration and conquest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colonization_of_the_Americas "Colonization of the Americas") of the [New World](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_World "New World"), completed the [first circumnavigation of the globe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_circumnavigation_of_the_globe "First circumnavigation of the globe"), and established [one of the largest empires in history](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), which spanned all continents and fostered a global trade system driven by [precious metals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Precious_metal "Precious metal"). In the 18th century, the [Nueva Planta decrees](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nueva_Planta_decrees "Nueva Planta decrees") centralised Spain under the Bourbons, strengthening royal authority. The 19th century witnessed the victorious [Peninsular War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War") (1808–1814) against Napoleonic forces and the [loss of most American colonies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_American_wars_of_independence "Spanish American wars of independence") amid liberal–absolutist conflicts. These struggles culminated in the [Spanish Civil War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") (1936–1939) and the [Francoist dictatorship](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francoist_Spain "Francoist Spain") (1939–1975). With the restoration of democracy and entry into the EU, Spain experienced [a major economic boom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_miracle "Spanish miracle") and social transformation. Since the [Spanish Golden Age](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Golden_Age "Spanish Golden Age") (_Siglo de Oro_), [Spanish culture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_culture "Spanish culture") has been influential worldwide, particularly in [Western Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") and the [Americas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Americas "Americas"). The [Spanish language](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") is spoken by more than 600 million [Hispanophones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hispanophone "Hispanophone"), making it the [world's second-most spoken native language](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_languages_by_number_of_native_speakers "List of languages by number of native speakers") and the most widely spoken [Romance language](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Romance_language "Romance language"). Spain is the [world's second-most visited country](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings"), [hosts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Spain "List of World Heritage Sites in Spain") one of the [largest numbers of World Heritage Sites](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites_by_country "World Heritage Sites by country"), and is the most popular destination for European students.

Spain is a secular [parliamentary democracy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") and a [constitutional monarchy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), with King [Felipe VI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Felipe_VI "Felipe VI") as [head of state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"). A [developed country](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country"), Spain has a [high nominal per capita income globally](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita"), and [its advanced economy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economy_of_Spain "Economy of Spain") ranks among the [largest in the world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") and the fourth-largest economy in the EU. Spain is considered a [cultural superpower](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Power_(international_relations)#Power_as_status "Power (international relations)")[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-25)[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-26)[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-27) with an influence that extends beyond its borders, and continues to promote its cultural value through participation in [multiple international organisations and forums](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Spain "Foreign relations of Spain"), as well as a key actor in the relations between Europe and [Latin America](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") due to its past history.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-28)[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-29)[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-30)

Etymology
---------

The name of Spain (_España_) comes from _[Hispania](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hispania "Hispania")_, the name used by the [Ancient Romans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Romans "Ancient Romans") for the [Iberian Peninsula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") during the [Roman Republic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") and the [Roman Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). The etymology of the term _Hispania_ remains uncertain.

The [Phoenicians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phoenicians "Phoenicians") referred to the region as _i-shphan-im_, possibly meaning 'land of [rabbits](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_rabbit "European rabbit") or [hyraxes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyraxes "Hyraxes")',[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-31) 'land of metals',[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-32)[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-Arechaga2009-33) or 'northern island'.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-34)[Roman coins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_coins "Roman coins") struck in the region from the reign of [Hadrian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hadrian "Hadrian") show a female figure with a rabbit at her feet,[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-burke-35) and [Strabo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") called it the 'land of the rabbits'.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-Spain-36)

History
-------

### Prehistory and Iberian peoples

[![Image 12: Settlement of Santa Trega's Castro's circular stone buildings, with a view of the mountain and the ocean](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/2014_Castro_de_Santa_Trega._Galiza-2.jpg/250px-2014_Castro_de_Santa_Trega._Galiza-2.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:2014_Castro_de_Santa_Trega._Galiza-2.jpg)

Celtic castro in Galicia

Archaeological research at [Atapuerca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Archaeological_site_of_Atapuerca "Archaeological site of Atapuerca") indicates the Iberian Peninsula was populated by [hominids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hominid "Hominid") 1.3 million years ago.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-37)

[Modern humans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human "Human") first arrived in Iberia from the north on foot about 35,000 years ago.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-38) The best-known artefacts of these prehistoric human settlements are the paintings in the [Altamira cave](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Altamira_(cave) "Altamira (cave)") of Cantabria in northern Iberia, which were created from 35,600 to 13,500 [BCE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Before_Common_Era "Before Common Era") by [Cro-Magnon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cro-Magnon "Cro-Magnon").[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-Science2012-39)[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-40) Archaeological and genetic evidence suggests that the Iberian Peninsula acted as one of several major refugia from which northern Europe was repopulated following the end of the [last ice age](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quaternary_glaciation "Quaternary glaciation").

The two largest groups inhabiting the Iberian Peninsula before the Roman conquest were the [Iberians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iberians "Iberians") and the [Celts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Celts "Celts").[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-41) The Iberians inhabited the Mediterranean side of the peninsula. The Celts inhabited much of the interior and Atlantic sides of the peninsula. [Basques](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basques "Basques") occupied the western area of the Pyrenees mountain range and adjacent areas. Phoenician-influenced [Tartessians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tartessos "Tartessos") flourished in the southwest. [Lusitanians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lusitanians "Lusitanians") and [Vettones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vettones "Vettones") occupied areas in the central west. Several cities were founded along the coast by [Phoenicians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia"). Trading outposts and colonies were established by [Greeks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greek_colonisation "Greek colonisation") in the East. Eventually, Phoenician-[Carthaginians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Carthage "Ancient Carthage") expanded inland towards the meseta. Due to the bellicose inland tribes, the Carthaginians settled on the coasts of the Iberian Peninsula.

### Roman Hispania and the Visigothic Kingdom

[![Image 13: Ancient Roman theatre of Mérida, Spain, showing the seating and stage area with columns and archways](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Teatro_de_M%C3%A9rida%2C_Espa%C3%B1a%2C_2017_18.jpg/250px-Teatro_de_M%C3%A9rida%2C_Espa%C3%B1a%2C_2017_18.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Teatro_de_M%C3%A9rida,_Espa%C3%B1a,_2017_18.jpg)

The [Roman Theatre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_Theatre_(M%C3%A9rida) "Roman Theatre (Mérida)") in [Mérida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M%C3%A9rida,_Spain "Mérida, Spain")

[![Image 14: Interior of the crypt of St. Isidore of Leon, Spain, with painted arches and decorated columns](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Leon_%28San_Isidoro%2C_pante%C3%B3n%29.jpg/250px-Leon_%28San_Isidoro%2C_pante%C3%B3n%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Leon_(San_Isidoro,_pante%C3%B3n).jpg)

Royal Panteon of San Isidoro, [Kingdom of León](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kingdom_of_Le%C3%B3n "Kingdom of León")

During the [Second Punic War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_Punic_War "Second Punic War"), roughly between 210 and 205 BCE, the expanding [Roman Republic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") captured Carthaginian trading colonies along the Mediterranean coast. It took the Romans nearly two centuries to complete the [conquest of the Iberian Peninsula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_the_Iberian_peninsula "Roman conquest of the Iberian peninsula"). They retained control of it for over six centuries. Roman rule was bound together by law, language, and the [Roman road](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_road "Roman road").[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-hispania-42) The cultures of the pre-Roman populations were gradually [Romanised](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Romanization_of_Hispania "Romanization of Hispania") (Latinised) at different rates depending on what part of the peninsula they lived in, with local leaders being admitted into the Roman aristocratic class.[[j]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-43)[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-country-44)

Hispania, the Roman name for the Iberian Peninsula, served as a granary for the Roman market, and its harbours exported gold, [wool](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wool "Wool"), [olive oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil"), and wine. Agricultural production increased with the introduction of irrigation projects, some of which remain in use. Emperors [Hadrian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hadrian "Hadrian"), [Trajan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trajan "Trajan"), [Theodosius I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theodosius_I "Theodosius I"), and the philosopher [Seneca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seneca_the_Younger "Seneca the Younger") were born in Hispania.[[k]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-45) Christianity was introduced into Hispania in the 1st century CE, and it became popular in the cities in the 2nd century.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-country-44) Most of Spain's present languages and religions, as well as the basis of its laws, originate from this period.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-hispania-42) Starting in 170 CE, incursions of North-African [Mauri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mauri "Mauri") in the province of [Baetica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hispania_Baetica "Hispania Baetica") took place.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-46)

The [Germanic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples")[Suebi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Suebi "Suebi") and [Vandals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vandals "Vandals"), together with the [Sarmatian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarmatian "Sarmatian")[Alans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alans "Alans"), entered the peninsula after 409, weakening the Western Roman Empire's jurisdiction over Hispania. The Suebi established a kingdom in north-western Iberia. The Vandals established themselves in the south of the peninsula by 420, before crossing over to North Africa in 429. As the western Roman empire disintegrated, the social and economic base became greatly simplified. The successor regimes maintained many of the institutions and laws of the late empire, including Christianity and assimilation into the evolving Roman culture.

The [Byzantines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") established an occidental province, [Spania](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spania "Spania"), in the south, with the intention of reviving Roman rule throughout Iberia. Eventually, however, Hispania was reunited under [Visigothic rule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visigothic_Kingdom "Visigothic Kingdom").

### Muslim era and _Reconquista_

From 711 to 718, as part of the expansion of the [Umayyad Caliphate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") which had [conquered North Africa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Maghreb "Muslim conquest of the Maghreb") from the [Byzantine Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), nearly all of the Iberian Peninsula was conquered by Muslims from across the Strait of Gibraltar, resulting in the collapse of the Visigothic Kingdom. Only a small area in the mountainous north of the peninsula stood out of the territory seized during the initial invasion. The [Kingdom of Asturias-León](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kingdom_of_Asturias "Kingdom of Asturias") consolidated upon this territory. Other Christian kingdoms, such as [Navarre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kingdom_of_Navarre "Kingdom of Navarre") and [Aragon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aragon "Kingdom of Aragon") in the mountainous north, eventually surged upon the consolidation of counties of the Carolingian _[Marca Hispanica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marca\_Hispanica "Marca Hispanica")_.[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-47) For several centuries, the fluctuating frontier between the Muslim and Christian-controlled areas of the peninsula was along the [Ebro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ebro "Ebro") and [Douro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Douro "Douro") valleys.

Conversion to [Islam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islam "Islam") proceeded at an increasing pace.
