The _[muladíes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muwallad "Muwallad")_ (Muslims of ethnic Iberian origin) are believed to have formed the majority of the population of Al-Andalus by the end of the 10th century.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-48)[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-chap2-49) A series of [Viking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Viking "Viking") incursions raided the coasts of the Iberian Peninsula in the 9th and 10th centuries.[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-50) The first recorded [Viking raid on Iberia took place in 844](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Viking_raid_on_Seville "Viking raid on Seville"). Seville was pillaged. It ended in failure with many Vikings killed by the Galicians' [ballistas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ballista "Ballista"). Seventy of the Vikings' longships captured on the beach and burned by the troops of King [Ramiro I of Asturias](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramiro_I_of_Asturias "Ramiro I of Asturias"). In the 11th century, the Caliphate of Córdoba collapsed, fracturing into a series of petty kingdoms (_[Taifas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taifa "Taifa")_),[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-51) often subject to the payment of a form of [protection money](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protection_racket "Protection racket") (_[Parias](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parias "Parias")_) to the Northern Christian kingdoms, which otherwise undertook a southward territorial expansion. The capture of the strategic city of [Toledo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toledo,_Spain "Toledo, Spain") in 1085 marked a significant shift in the balance of power in favour of the Christian kingdoms.[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-52) The arrival from North Africa of the Islamic ruling sects of the [Almoravids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almoravids "Almoravids") and the [Almohads](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almohads "Almohads") achieved temporary unity upon the Muslim-ruled territory, with a stricter, less tolerant application of Islam, and partially reversed some Christian territorial gains.

[![Image 15: Portrait of a couple; the man wears a black doublet with gold chain, while the woman wears a burgundy dress with white square collar and a head covering](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Ferdinand_of_Aragon%2C_Isabella_of_Castile_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Ferdinand_of_Aragon%2C_Isabella_of_Castile_%28cropped%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ferdinand_of_Aragon,_Isabella_of_Castile_(cropped).jpg)

[Catholic Monarchs of Spain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catholic_Monarchs_of_Spain "Catholic Monarchs of Spain")

The [Kingdom of León](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kingdom_of_Le%C3%B3n "Kingdom of León") was the strongest Christian kingdom for centuries. In 1188, the first form (restricted to the bishops, the magnates, and 'the elected citizens of each city') of modern parliamentary session in Europe was held in [León](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Le%C3%B3n_(Spain) "León (Spain)") ([Cortes of León](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cortes_of_Le%C3%B3n "Cortes of León")).[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-53) The [Kingdom of Castile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kingdom_of_Castile "Kingdom of Castile"), formed from Leonese territory, was its successor as the strongest kingdom. The kings and the nobility fought for power and influence in this period. The example of the Roman emperors influenced the political objective of the Crown, while the nobles benefited from [feudalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism").

Muslim strongholds in the [Guadalquivir Valley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guadalquivir_Valley "Guadalquivir Valley") such as Córdoba (1236) and [Seville](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seville "Seville") (1248) fell to Castile in the 13th century. The [County of Barcelona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/County_of_Barcelona "County of Barcelona") and the [Kingdom of Aragon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aragon "Kingdom of Aragon") entered in a dynastic union and gained territory and power in the Mediterranean. In 1229, [Mallorca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mallorca "Mallorca") was conquered, as was [Valencia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Valencia "Valencia") in 1238. In the 13th and 14th centuries, the North-African [Marinids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marinid "Marinid") established some enclaves around the Strait of Gibraltar. Upon the conclusion of the [Granada War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Granada_War "Granada War"), the [Nasrid Sultanate of Granada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kingdom_of_Granada "Kingdom of Granada") (the remaining Muslim-ruled polity in the Iberian Peninsula after 1246) capitulated in 1492 to the military strength of the [Catholic Monarchs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catholic_Monarchs "Catholic Monarchs"), and it was integrated from then on in the Crown of Castile.[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-54)

### Spanish Empire

[![Image 16: The painter painted the port of Seville in an impressionistic manner](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/La_sevilla_del_sigloXVI.jpg/330px-La_sevilla_del_sigloXVI.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:La_sevilla_del_sigloXVI.jpg)

Late 16th-century Seville, the harbor enjoying the exclusive right to trade with the New World

In 1469, the crowns of the Christian kingdoms of Castile and Aragon were united by the marriage of their monarchs, Isabella I and Ferdinand II, respectively. In 1492, as part of the [Spanish Inquisition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Inquisition "Spanish Inquisition"), Jews were forced to choose between conversion to Catholicism or expulsion;[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-55) as many as 200,000 Jews were [expelled from Castile and Aragon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Expulsion_of_Jews_from_Spain "Expulsion of Jews from Spain"). The year 1492 also marked the arrival of [Christopher Columbus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") in the [New World](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_World "New World"), during a voyage funded by Isabella. Columbus's first voyage crossed the Atlantic and reached the Caribbean Islands, beginning the European exploration and conquest of the Americas.

The [Treaty of Granada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Treaty_of_Granada "Treaty of Granada") guaranteed religious tolerance towards Muslims,[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-56) for a few years before Islam was outlawed in 1502 in Castile and 1527 in Aragon, leading the remaining Muslim population to become nominally Christian _[Moriscos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morisco "Morisco")_. About four decades after the [War of the Alpujarras](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/War_of_the_Alpujarras "War of the Alpujarras") (1568–1571), over 300,000 [_moriscos_ were expelled](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Expulsion_of_the_Moriscos "Expulsion of the Moriscos"), settling primarily in North Africa.[[46]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-cong-57)

[![Image 17: Map of the world showing the territory of the Spanish Empire](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Spanish_Empire_%28diachronic%29.svg/330px-Spanish_Empire_%28diachronic%29.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Spanish_Empire_(diachronic).svg)

A diachronic map of the Spanish Empire

The unification of the crowns of Aragon and Castile by the marriage of their sovereigns laid the basis for modern Spain and the Spanish Empire, although each kingdom of Spain remained a separate country socially, politically, legally, and in currency and language.[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-58)[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-59)

[Habsburg Spain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain") was one of the leading world powers throughout the 16th century and most of the 17th century, a position reinforced by trade and wealth from colonial possessions and became the world's leading [maritime power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Navy "Spanish Navy"). It reached its apogee during the reigns of the first two Spanish Habsburgs—[Charles V/I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") (1516–1556) and [Philip II](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") (1556–1598). This period saw the [Spanish conquest of the Aztec](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Aztec_Empire "Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire") and [Inca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Inca_Empire "Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire") empires, the [Italian Wars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Italian_Wars "Italian Wars"), the [Schmalkaldic War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schmalkaldic_War "Schmalkaldic War"), the [Dutch Revolt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dutch_Revolt "Dutch Revolt"), the [War of the Portuguese Succession](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/War_of_the_Portuguese_Succession "War of the Portuguese Succession"), clashes with the [Ottomans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ottoman%E2%80%93Habsburg_wars "Ottoman–Habsburg wars"), intervention in the [French Wars of Religion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion"), and the [Anglo-Spanish War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1585%E2%80%931604) "Anglo-Spanish War (1585–1604)").[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-60)

[![Image 18: The image depicts a world map tracing the Spanish and Portuguese routes of exploration and trade](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Principales_Rutas_Comerciales_del_Imperio_Espa%C3%B1ol.jpg/330px-Principales_Rutas_Comerciales_del_Imperio_Espa%C3%B1ol.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Principales_Rutas_Comerciales_del_Imperio_Espa%C3%B1ol.jpg)

Main trade routes of the Spanish Empire

Through exploration and conquest or royal marriage alliances and inheritance, the [Spanish Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") expanded across vast areas in the Americas, the Indo-Pacific, Africa as well as the European continent, including holdings in the Italian Peninsula, the [Low Countries](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries") and the [Franche-Comté](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franche-Comt%C3%A9 "Franche-Comté"). The so-called [Age of Discovery](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") featured explorations by sea and by land, the opening-up of new [trade routes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trade_route "Trade route") across oceans, conquests and the beginnings of European [colonialism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism"). [Precious metals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Precious_metal "Precious metal"), spices, luxuries, and previously unknown plants brought to the metropole played a leading part in transforming the European understanding of the globe.[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-61)

[![Image 19: Plaza Mayor, Madrid's central plaza, is a large brick-lined square surrounded by ornate buildings with arched walkways and lit by old-fashioned lampposts](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Plaza_Mayor_de_Madrid_06.jpg/330px-Plaza_Mayor_de_Madrid_06.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Plaza_Mayor_de_Madrid_06.jpg)

Plaza Mayor of Madrid built in 1619 during the reign of Philip III.

The cultural efflorescence witnessed during this period is now referred to as the [Spanish Golden Age](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Golden_Age "Spanish Golden Age"). The expansion of the empire caused immense upheaval in the Americas as the collapse of societies and empires and new diseases from Europe devastated American indigenous populations. The rise of [humanism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Humanism "Humanism"), the [Counter-Reformation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation") and new geographical discoveries and conquests raised issues that were addressed by the intellectual movement now known as the [School of Salamanca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/School_of_Salamanca "School of Salamanca"), which developed the first modern theories of what are now known as [international law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_law "International law") and human rights. Spain's 16th-century maritime supremacy was demonstrated by the victory over the [Ottoman Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") at the [Battle of Lepanto](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Lepanto "Battle of Lepanto") in 1571 and over Portugal at the [Battle of Ponta Delgada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Vila_Franca_do_Campo "Battle of Vila Franca do Campo") in 1582, and then after the setback of the [Spanish Armada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada") in 1588, in a series of victories against [England](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/England "England") in the [Anglo-Spanish War of 1585–1604](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1585%E2%80%931604) "Anglo-Spanish War (1585–1604)"). In the middle decades of the 17th century, Spain's maritime power went into a long decline with mounting defeats against the [Dutch Republic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") ([Battle of the Downs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_the_Downs "Battle of the Downs")) and then England in the [Anglo-Spanish War of 1654–1660](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1654%E2%80%931660) "Anglo-Spanish War (1654–1660)"). By the 1660s, Spain was struggling to defend its overseas possessions from pirates and privateers.

The [Protestant Reformation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") increased Spain's involvement in religiously charged wars, forcing ever-expanding military efforts across Europe and in the Mediterranean.[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-62) By the middle decades of a war- and [plague](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Plague_of_Seville "Great Plague of Seville")-ridden 17th-century Europe, the Spanish Habsburgs had enmeshed Spain in continent-wide religious-political conflicts. These conflicts drained Spain of resources and undermined the economy generally. Spain managed to hold on to most of the scattered Habsburg empire, and help the imperial forces of the [Holy Roman Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") reverse a large part of the advances made by Protestant forces.[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-Payne_1973-63)

Spain was finally forced to recognise the [separation of Portugal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War") and the United Provinces (Dutch Republic), and eventually suffered some serious military reverses to France in the latter stages of the immensely destructive, Europe-wide [Thirty Years' War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War").[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-Payne_1973-63) In the latter half of the 17th century, Spain went into a gradual decline, during which it surrendered several small territories to France and England; however, it maintained and enlarged its vast overseas empire, which remained intact until the beginning of the 19th century.

#### 18th century

[![Image 20: Oil painting depicting The Family of Philip V, painted by Louis Michel van Loo in 1743, exhibiting a group of nobles in a large palace, clad in the fashion of the time](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/La_familia_de_Felipe_V_%28Van_Loo%29.jpg/250px-La_familia_de_Felipe_V_%28Van_Loo%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:La_familia_de_Felipe_V_(Van_Loo).jpg)

[The family of Philip V](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Family_of_Philip_V_(1743) "The Family of Philip V (1743)"). During the [Enlightenment in Spain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enlightenment_in_Spain "Enlightenment in Spain") a new royal family reigned, the [House of Bourbon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/House_of_Bourbon "House of Bourbon").

The decline culminated in a controversy over succession to the throne, which consumed the first years of the 18th century. The [War of the Spanish Succession](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") was a wide-ranging international conflict combined with a civil war, and cost the kingdom its European possessions and its position as a leading European power.[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-64)

During this war, a new dynasty originating in France, the [Bourbons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/House_of_Bourbon "House of Bourbon"), was installed. The Crowns of Castile and Aragon had been long united only by the Monarchy and the common institution of the Inquisition's [Holy Office](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Inquisition "Spanish Inquisition").[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-65) A number of reform policies, the so-called [Bourbon Reforms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bourbon_Reforms "Bourbon Reforms"), were pursued by the Monarchy with the overarching goal of centralised authority and administrative uniformity.[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-66) They included the abolishment of many of the old regional privileges and laws,[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-67) as well as the customs barrier between the Crowns of Aragon and Castile in 1717, followed by the introduction of new property taxes in the Aragonese kingdoms.[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-68)

The 18th century saw a gradual recovery and an increase in prosperity through much of the Spanish empire. The predominant economic policy was an interventionist one, and the State also pursued policies aiming towards infrastructure development as well as the abolition of internal customs and the reduction of export tariffs.[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-69) Projects of agricultural colonisation with new settlements took place in the south of mainland Spain.[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMart%C3%ADnez_Shaw201614,_23-70)[Enlightenment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enlightenment_in_Spain "Enlightenment in Spain") ideas began to gain ground among some of the kingdom's elite and monarchy.

### Liberalism and nation state

[![Image 21: Monument to the Constitution of 1812, also known as the Monument to the Cortes of Cadiz](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Monumento_a_la_Constituci%C3%B3n_de_1812%2C_C%C3%A1diz%2C_Espa%C3%B1a%2C_2015-12-08%2C_DD_80.JPG/250px-Monumento_a_la_Constituci%C3%B3n_de_1812%2C_C%C3%A1diz%2C_Espa%C3%B1a%2C_2015-12-08%2C_DD_80.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Monumento_a_la_Constituci%C3%B3n_de_1812,_C%C3%A1diz,_Espa%C3%B1a,_2015-12-08,_DD_80.JPG)

[Monument to the Constitution of 1812](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monument_to_the_Constitution_of_1812 "Monument to the Constitution of 1812") in [Cádiz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C%C3%A1diz "Cádiz").

[![Image 22: A painting depicting the execution of Torrijos and his companions on the beach of Málaga, Spain](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Fusilamiento_de_Torrijos_%28Gisbert%29.jpg/250px-Fusilamiento_de_Torrijos_%28Gisbert%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Fusilamiento_de_Torrijos_(Gisbert).jpg)

[Execution of Torrijos and his men](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Execution_of_Torrijos_and_his_Companions_on_the_Beach_at_M%C3%A1laga "Execution of Torrijos and his Companions on the Beach at Málaga") in 1831, [Málaga](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M%C3%A1laga "Málaga"). Ferdinand VII took [repressive measures](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ominous_Decade "Ominous Decade") against the liberal forces in his country.

[![Image 23: People casting votes, in 1933 Spanish elections](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/01_eibar.jpg/250px-01_eibar.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:01_eibar.jpg)

Women voting in [Éibar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%89ibar "Éibar") in 1933, after [women's suffrage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage") was approved. Deputy [Clara Campoamor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clara_Campoamor "Clara Campoamor") was a key figure for the right to be granted.

In 1793, Spain went to war against the revolutionary new [French Republic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_French_Republic "First French Republic") as a member of [the first Coalition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/War_of_the_First_Coalition "War of the First Coalition"). The subsequent [War of the Pyrenees](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/War_of_the_Pyrenees "War of the Pyrenees") polarised the country in a reaction against the [gallicised](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francization "Francization") elites and following defeat in the field, peace was made with France in 1795 at the [Peace of Basel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peace_of_Basel "Peace of Basel") in which Spain lost control over two-thirds of the island of [Hispaniola](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola"). In 1807, a secret treaty between [Napoleon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Napoleon_I_of_France "Napoleon I of France") and the unpopular prime minister led to a new declaration of war against Britain and Portugal. French troops entered the country to invade Portugal but instead occupied Spain's major fortresses. The Spanish king abdicated and a puppet kingdom satellite to the French Empire was installed with [Joseph Bonaparte](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joseph_Bonaparte "Joseph Bonaparte") as king.

The [2 May 1808 revolt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dos_de_Mayo_Uprising "Dos de Mayo Uprising") was one of many uprisings across the country against the French occupation.[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-71) These revolts marked the beginning of a devastating [war of independence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War") against the Napoleonic regime.[[61]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGates200120-72) Further military action by Spanish armies, [guerrilla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guerrilla "Guerrilla") warfare and an Anglo-Portuguese allied army, combined with [Napoleon's failure on the Russian front](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Napoleon%27s_Invasion_of_Russia "Napoleon's Invasion of Russia"), led to the retreat of French imperial armies from the Iberian Peninsula in 1814, and the return of [King Ferdinand VII](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferdinand_VII_of_Spain "Ferdinand VII of Spain").[[62]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGates2001467-73)

During the war, in 1810, a revolutionary body, the [Cortes of Cádiz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cortes_of_C%C3%A1diz "Cortes of Cádiz"), was assembled to coordinate the effort against the Bonapartist regime and to prepare a constitution.[[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-74) It met as one body, and its members represented the entire Spanish empire.[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-75) In 1812, a [constitution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Constitution_of_1812 "Spanish Constitution of 1812") for universal representation under a constitutional monarchy was declared, but after the fall of the Bonapartist regime, the Spanish king dismissed the Cortes Generales, set on ruling as an [absolute monarch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy").

The French occupation of mainland Spain created an opportunity for overseas [_criollo_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Criollo_people "Criollo people") elites who resented the privilege towards [Peninsular elites](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peninsulars "Peninsulars") and demanded [retroversion of the sovereignty to the people](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Retroversion_of_the_sovereignty_to_the_people "Retroversion of the sovereignty to the people"). Starting in 1809 the American colonies began a series of revolutions and declared independence, leading to the [Spanish American wars of independence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_American_wars_of_independence "Spanish American wars of independence") that put an end to the metropole's grip over the [Spanish Main](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Main "Spanish Main"). [Attempts to re-assert control](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reconquista_(Spanish_America) "Reconquista (Spanish America)") proved futile with opposition not only in the colonies but also in the Iberian peninsula and army revolts followed. By the end of 1826, the only American colonies Spain held were [Cuba](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Cuba "Captaincy General of Cuba") and [Puerto Rico](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Puerto_Rico "Captaincy General of Puerto Rico"). The Napoleonic War left Spain economically ruined, deeply divided and politically unstable. In the 1830s and 1840s, [Carlism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carlism "Carlism") (a reactionary legitimist movement supportive of an alternative Bourbon branch), fought against the government forces supportive of Queen [Isabella II](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isabella_II_of_Spain "Isabella II of Spain")'s dynastic rights in the [Carlist Wars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carlist_Wars "Carlist Wars"). Government forces prevailed, but the conflict between [_progressives_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Progressive_Party_(Spain) "Progressive Party (Spain)") and _[moderates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moderate\_Party\_(Spain) "Moderate Party (Spain)")_ ended in a weak early constitutional period. The 1868 [Glorious Revolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glorious_Revolution_(Spain) "Glorious Revolution (Spain)") was followed by the 1868–1874 progressive _[Sexenio Democrático](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexenio\_Democr%C3%A1tico "Sexenio Democrático")_ (including the short-lived [First Spanish Republic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_Spanish_Republic "First Spanish Republic")), which yielded to a stable monarchic period, the [Restoration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Restoration_(Spain) "Restoration (Spain)") (1875–1931).[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-76)

In the late 19th century nationalist movements arose in the Philippines and Cuba. In 1895 and 1896 the [Cuban War of Independence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cuban_War_of_Independence "Cuban War of Independence") and the [Philippine Revolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philippine_Revolution "Philippine Revolution") broke out and eventually the United States became involved. The [Spanish–American War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish%E2%80%93American_War "Spanish–American War") was fought from April to August 1898 and resulted in Spain losing the last of its once vast colonial empire outside of North Africa. _El Desastre_ (the Disaster), as the war became known in Spain, gave added impetus to the [Generation of '98](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Generation_of_%2798 "Generation of '98"). Although the period around the turn of the century was one of increasing prosperity, the 20th century brought little social peace. Spain played a minor part in the [scramble for Africa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scramble_for_Africa "Scramble for Africa"). It remained neutral [during World War I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain_during_World_War_I "Spain during World War I"). The heavy losses suffered by the colonial troops in conflicts in northern Morocco against Riffians forces brought discredit to the government and undermined the monarchy.

Industrialisation, the development of railways and incipient capitalism developed in several areas of the country, particularly in [Barcelona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), as well as [labour movement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Labour_movement "Labour movement") and socialist and anarchist ideas. The [1870 Barcelona Workers' Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1870_Barcelona_Workers%27_Congress "1870 Barcelona Workers' Congress") and the [1888 Barcelona Universal Exposition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1888_Barcelona_Universal_Exposition "1888 Barcelona Universal Exposition") are good examples of this. In 1879, the [Spanish Socialist Workers' Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Socialist_Workers%27_Party "Spanish Socialist Workers' Party") was founded. A trade union linked to this party, [Unión General de Trabajadores](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uni%C3%B3n_General_de_Trabajadores "Unión General de Trabajadores"), was founded in 1888. In the anarcho-syndicalist trend of the labour movement in Spain, [Confederación Nacional del Trabajo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Confederaci%C3%B3n_Nacional_del_Trabajo "Confederación Nacional del Trabajo") was founded in 1910 and [Federación Anarquista Ibérica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federaci%C3%B3n_Anarquista_Ib%C3%A9rica "Federación Anarquista Ibérica") in 1927. Catalanism and Vasquism, alongside other nationalisms and regionalisms in Spain, arose in that period: the [Basque Nationalist Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basque_Nationalist_Party "Basque Nationalist Party") formed in 1895 and [Regionalist League of Catalonia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regionalist_League_of_Catalonia "Regionalist League of Catalonia") in 1901. Political corruption and repression weakened the democratic system of the constitutional monarchy of a two-parties system.[[66]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-77) The July 1909 [Tragic Week](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tragic_Week_(Spain) "Tragic Week (Spain)") events and repression exemplified the social instability of the time. The [La Canadiense strike](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/La_Canadiense_strike "La Canadiense strike") in 1919 led to the first law limiting the working day to eight hours.[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-78)

After a period of Crown-supported dictatorship from 1923 to 1931, the first elections since 1923, largely understood as a plebiscite on Monarchy, took place: the [12 April 1931 municipal elections](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1931_Spanish_local_elections "1931 Spanish local elections"). These gave a resounding victory to the Republican-Socialist candidacies in large cities and provincial capitals, with a majority of monarchist councilors in rural areas. The king left the country and the proclamation of the Republic on 14 April ensued, with the formation of a provisional government. A [constitution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Constitution_of_1931 "Spanish Constitution of 1931") for the country was passed in October 1931 following the [June 1931 Constituent general election](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1931_Spanish_general_election "1931 Spanish general election"), and a series of cabinets presided by [Manuel Azaña](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manuel_Aza%C3%B1a "Manuel Azaña") supported by republican parties and the [PSOE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Socialist_Workers%27_Party "Spanish Socialist Workers' Party") followed. In the election held in 1933 the right triumphed and in 1936, the left. During the [Second Republic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") there was a great political and social upheaval, marked by a sharp radicalisation of the left and the right. Instances of political violence during this period included the burning of churches, the [1932 failed coup d'état led by José Sanjurjo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanjurjada "Sanjurjada"), the [Revolution of 1934](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Revolution_of_1934 "Revolution of 1934") and numerous attacks against rival political leaders. On the other hand, it is also during the Second Republic when important reforms to modernise the country were initiated: a democratic constitution, agrarian reform, restructuring of the army, political decentralisation and [women's right to vote](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage").

### Civil War and Francoist dictatorship

[![Image 24: Group of men standing in uniform in the street](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Reemplazo_republicano.jpg/250px-Reemplazo_republicano.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Reemplazo_republicano.jpg)

Republican volunteers at [Teruel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Teruel "Teruel"), 1936

[![Image 25: A row of German soldiers with rifles stand at attention as officers pass by during the Nazi regime](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/96/Meeting_at_Hendaye_%28en.wiki%29.jpg/250px-Meeting_at_Hendaye_%28en.wiki%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Meeting_at_Hendaye_(en.wiki).jpg)

Spanish leader [Francisco Franco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") and [Adolf Hitler](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") at the [Meeting at Hendaye](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meeting_at_Hendaye "Meeting at Hendaye"), 1940

The Spanish Civil War broke out in 1936: on 17 and 18 July, part of the military [carried out a coup d'état](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_coup_of_July_1936 "Spanish coup of July 1936") that triumphed in only part of the country. The situation led to a civil war, in which the territory was divided into two zones: one [under the authority of the Republican government](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Republican_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Republican faction (Spanish Civil War)"), that counted on outside support from the [Soviet Union](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and [Mexico](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") (and from [International Brigades](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_response_to_the_Spanish_Civil_War#International_Brigades "International response to the Spanish Civil War")), and the other controlled by the putschists (the [Nationalist or rebel faction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nationalist_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Nationalist faction (Spanish Civil War)")), most critically supported by [Nazi Germany](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Fascist Italy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fascist_Italy_(1922%E2%80%931943) "Fascist Italy (1922–1943)"). The Republic was not supported by the Western powers due to the British-led policy of [non-intervention](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-intervention "Non-intervention"). General [Francisco Franco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") was sworn in as the supreme leader of the rebels on 1 October 1936. An uneasy relationship between the Republican government and the grassroots anarchists who had initiated a partial [social revolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Revolution_of_1936 "Spanish Revolution of 1936") also ensued.

The civil war was viciously fought and there were [many atrocities committed by all sides](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War#Atrocities "Spanish Civil War"). The [war](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") claimed the lives of over 500,000 people and caused the flight of up to a half-million citizens from the country.[[68]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-79)[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-80) On 1 April 1939, five months before the beginning of [World War II](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the rebel side led by Franco emerged victorious, imposing a dictatorship over the whole country. Thousands were imprisoned after the civil war in [Francoist concentration camps](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francoist_concentration_camps "Francoist concentration camps").

The regime remained nominally "[neutral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neutrality_(international_relations) "Neutrality (international relations)")" for much of the Second World War, although it was [sympathetic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain_in_World_War_II "Spain in World War II") to [the Axis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Axis_Powers "Axis Powers") and provided the Nazi [Wehrmacht](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht") with [Spanish volunteers in the Eastern Front](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blue_Division "Blue Division"). The only legal party under Franco's dictatorship was the [Falange Española Tradicionalista y de las JONS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Falange_Espa%C3%B1ola_Tradicionalista_y_de_las_JONS "Falange Española Tradicionalista y de las JONS") (FET y de las JONS), formed in 1937 upon the merging of the Fascist [Falange Española de las JONS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Falange_Espa%C3%B1ola_de_las_JONS "Falange Española de las JONS") and the Carlist traditionalists and to which the rest of right-wing groups supporting the rebels also added. The name of "[Movimiento Nacional](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Movimiento_Nacional "Movimiento Nacional")", sometimes understood as a wider structure than the FET y de las JONS proper, largely imposed over the later's name in official documents along the 1950s.

After the war Spain was politically and economically isolated, and was kept out of the United Nations. This changed in 1955, during the [Cold War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") period, when it became strategically important for the US to establish a military presence on the Iberian Peninsula as a counter to any possible move by the Soviet Union into the Mediterranean basin. US Cold War strategic priorities included the dissemination of American educational ideas to foster modernisation and expansion.[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-81) In the 1960s, Spain registered an [unprecedented rate of economic growth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_miracle "Spanish miracle") which was propelled by [industrialisation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_miracle#Industrialization "Spanish miracle"), a mass internal migration from rural areas to [Madrid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), [Barcelona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona") and the [Basque Country](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community) "Basque Country (autonomous community)") and the creation of a mass tourism industry. Franco's rule was also characterised by [authoritarianism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francoist_Spain#Fascism_and_authoritarianism "Francoist Spain"), [promotion of a unitary national identity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francoist_Spain#Spanish_nationalism "Francoist Spain"), [National Catholicism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Catholicism "National Catholicism"), and [discriminatory language policies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Language_policies_of_Francoist_Spain "Language policies of Francoist Spain").

### Restoration of democracy

[![Image 26: King Juan Carlos I is depicted in an elevated view during a formal address to a large assembly in a chamber, surrounded by dignitaries and officials in suits and dresses](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/De_proclamatie_en_be%C3%ABdiging_van_Prins_Juan_Carlos_tot_Koning_van_Spanje_tijdens_%2C_Bestanddeelnr_254-9763.jpg/250px-De_proclamatie_en_be%C3%ABdiging_van_Prins_Juan_Carlos_tot_Koning_van_Spanje_tijdens_%2C_Bestanddeelnr_254-9763.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:De_proclamatie_en_be%C3%ABdiging_van_Prins_Juan_Carlos_tot_Koning_van_Spanje_tijdens_,_Bestanddeelnr_254-9763.jpg)

Juan Carlos I before the Cortes Españolas, during his proclamation as King on 22 November 1975

[![Image 27: Pictured is the signing of the Schengen Agreement in 1985, attended by three men in suits with flags in the background](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Felipe_Gonz%C3%A1lez_firma_el_Tratado_de_Adhesi%C3%B3n_de_Espa%C3%B1a_a_la_Comunidad_Econ%C3%B3mica_Europea_en_el_Palacio_Real_de_Madrid._Pool_Moncloa._12_de_junio_de_1985.jpeg/250px-Felipe_Gonz%C3%A1lez_firma_el_Tratado_de_Adhesi%C3%B3n_de_Espa%C3%B1a_a_la_Comunidad_Econ%C3%B3mica_Europea_en_el_Palacio_Real_de_Madrid._Pool_Moncloa._12_de_junio_de_1985.jpeg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Felipe_Gonz%C3%A1lez_firma_el_Tratado_de_Adhesi%C3%B3n_de_Espa%C3%B1a_a_la_Comunidad_Econ%C3%B3mica_Europea_en_el_Palacio_Real_de_Madrid._Pool_Moncloa._12_de_junio_de_1985.jpeg)

[Felipe González](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Felipe_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Felipe González") signing the treaty of accession to the [European Economic Community](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") on 12 June 1985

In 1962, a group of politicians involved in the opposition to Franco's regime inside the country and in exile met in the congress of the [European Movement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Movement "European Movement") in Munich, where they made a resolution in favour of democracy.[[71]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-82)[[72]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-83)[[73]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-84) With Franco's death in November 1975, [Juan Carlos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Juan_Carlos_I_of_Spain "Juan Carlos I of Spain") succeeded to the position of [King of Spain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/King_of_Spain "King of Spain") and [head of state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") in accordance with the Francoist law. With the approval of the new [Spanish Constitution of 1978](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Constitution_of_1978 "Spanish Constitution of 1978") and the [restoration of democracy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_transition_to_democracy "Spanish transition to democracy"), the State [devolved](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") much authority to the regions and created an internal organisation based on [autonomous communities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autonomous_communities_of_Spain "Autonomous communities of Spain"). The [Spanish 1977 Amnesty Law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_1977_Amnesty_Law "Spanish 1977 Amnesty Law") let people of Franco's regime continue inside institutions without consequences, even perpetrators of some crimes during transition to democracy like the [Massacre of 3 March 1976 in Vitoria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Massacre_of_3_March_1976_in_Vitoria "Massacre of 3 March 1976 in Vitoria") or [1977 Massacre of Atocha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1977_Massacre_of_Atocha "1977 Massacre of Atocha").

In the Basque Country, moderate [Basque nationalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basque_nationalism "Basque nationalism") coexisted with a [radical nationalist movement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basque_Conflict "Basque Conflict") led by the armed organisation [ETA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ETA_(separatist_group) "ETA (separatist group)") until the latter's dissolution in May 2018.[[74]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-85) The group was formed in 1959 during Franco's rule but had continued to wage its violent campaign even after the restoration of democracy and the return of a large measure of regional autonomy.

On 23 February 1981, rebel elements among the security forces seized the Cortes in an attempt to impose [a military-backed government](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/23-F "23-F"). King Juan Carlos took personal command of the military and successfully ordered the coup plotters, via national television, to surrender.[[75]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-86)

During the 1980s the democratic restoration made possible a growing open society. New cultural movements based on freedom appeared, like [La Movida Madrileña](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/La_Movida_Madrile%C3%B1a "La Movida Madrileña"). In May 1982 Spain joined [NATO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NATO "NATO"), followed by [a referendum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1986_Spanish_NATO_membership_referendum "1986 Spanish NATO membership referendum") after a strong social opposition. That year the [Spanish Socialist Workers Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Socialist_Workers_Party "Spanish Socialist Workers Party") (PSOE) came to power, the first left-wing government in 43 years. In 1986 Spain joined the [European Economic Community](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community"), which later became the [European Union](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). The PSOE was replaced in government by the [Partido Popular](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/People%27s_Party_(Spain) "People's Party (Spain)") (PP) in 1996 after scandals around participation of the government of [Felipe González](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Felipe_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Felipe González") in the [Dirty war against ETA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GAL_(paramilitary_group) "GAL (paramilitary group)").

### Economic prosperity and crash

On 1 January 2002, Spain fully adopted the [euro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Euro "Euro"), and Spain experienced strong economic growth, well above the EU average during the early 2000s. However, well-publicised concerns issued by many economic commentators at the height of the boom warned that extraordinary property prices and a high foreign trade deficit were likely to lead to a painful economic collapse.[[76]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-87)

[![Image 28: Thousands of people crowd a public square at night, surrounded by illuminated buildings](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Madrid_October15.jpg/250px-Madrid_October15.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Madrid_October15.jpg)

Demonstration against the crisis and high youth unemployment in Madrid, 15 October 2011

In 2002, the [Prestige oil spill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prestige_oil_spill "Prestige oil spill") occurred with big ecological consequences along Spain's Atlantic coastline. In 2003 [José María Aznar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Aznar "José María Aznar") supported US president [George W. Bush](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") in the [Iraq War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"), and a strong movement against war rose in Spanish society. In March 2004 a local [Islamist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamist "Islamist") terrorist group inspired by [Al-Qaeda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") carried out the largest terrorist attack in Western European history when they killed 191 people and wounded more than 1,800 others by [bombing commuter trains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2004_Madrid_train_bombings "2004 Madrid train bombings") in Madrid.[[77]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-88) Though initial suspicions focused on the Basque terrorist group [ETA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ETA_(separatist_group) "ETA (separatist group)"), evidence of Islamist involvement soon emerged. Because of the proximity of the [2004 Spanish general election](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2004_Spanish_general_election "2004 Spanish general election"), the issue of responsibility quickly became a political controversy, with the main competing parties PP and PSOE exchanging accusations over the handling of the incident.[[78]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-89) The PSOE won the election, led by [José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Luis_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Zapatero "José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero").[[79]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-90)

In the early 2000s, the proportion of [Spain's foreign born population](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immigration_to_Spain#Currently "Immigration to Spain") increased rapidly during its economic boom but then declined due to the [2008 financial crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis").[[80]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-91) In 2005, the Spanish government legalised [same sex marriage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Spain "Same-sex marriage in Spain"), becoming the third country worldwide to do so.[[81]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-92) Decentralisation was supported with much resistance of Constitutional Court and conservative opposition, so did gender politics like quotas or the law against gender violence. Government talks with ETA happened, and the group announced its permanent cease of violence in 2010.[[82]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-93)

The bursting of the [Spanish property bubble](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_property_bubble "Spanish property bubble") in 2008 led to the [2008–2014 Spanish financial crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008%E2%80%932014_Spanish_financial_crisis "2008–2014 Spanish financial crisis"). High levels of unemployment, cuts in government spending and corruption in [Royal family](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Royal_family "Royal family") and [People's Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/People%27s_Party_(Spain) "People's Party (Spain)") served as a backdrop to the [2011–12 Spanish protests](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Spanish_protests "2011–12 Spanish protests").[[83]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-94)[Catalan independentism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catalan_independentism "Catalan independentism") also rose. In 2011, [Mariano Rajoy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mariano_Rajoy "Mariano Rajoy")'s conservative [People's Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/People%27s_Party_(Spain) "People's Party (Spain)") won the election with 44.6% of votes.[[84]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-95) As prime minister, he implemented austerity measures for EU bailout, the EU Stability and Growth Pact.[[85]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-96) On 19 June 2014, the monarch, Juan Carlos, abdicated in favour of his son, who became [Felipe VI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Felipe_VI "Felipe VI").[[86]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-97)

### Constitutional issues

[![Image 29: A polling place with many people and a man is placing a ballot into a box](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/01.10.2017_Referendum_1-OCT_%284%29.jpg/250px-01.10.2017_Referendum_1-OCT_%284%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:01.10.2017_Referendum_1-OCT_(4).jpg)

Catalans casting their votes in [Barcelona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona") during the [2017 Catalan independence referendum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2017_Catalan_independence_referendum "2017 Catalan independence referendum")

In October 2017 a [Catalan independence referendum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2017_Catalan_independence_referendum "2017 Catalan independence referendum") was held and the [Catalan parliament](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catalan_parliament "Catalan parliament") voted to unilaterally declare [independence from Spain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2017_Catalonia_declaration_of_independence "2017 Catalonia declaration of independence") to form a Catalan Republic[[87]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-98)[[88]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-99) on the day the [Spanish Senate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Senate_of_Spain "Senate of Spain") was discussing approving direct rule over Catalonia as called for by the Spanish Prime Minister.[[89]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-BBC-27Oct17-1-100)[[90]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-BBC-27Oct17-2-101) On the same day the Senate granted the power to impose direct rule and Rajoy dissolved the Catalan parliament and called a new election.[[91]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-102) No country recognised Catalonia as a separate state.[[92]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-EuN_20171027-103)

In June 2018, the [Congress of Deputies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Congress_of_Deputies "Congress of Deputies") passed a [motion of no-confidence against Rajoy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2018_vote_of_no_confidence_in_the_government_of_Mariano_Rajoy "2018 vote of no confidence in the government of Mariano Rajoy") and replaced him with the PSOE leader [Pedro Sánchez](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pedro_S%C3%A1nchez_(politician) "Pedro Sánchez (politician)").[[93]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-104) In 2019, the first ever coalition government in Spain was formed, between PSOE and Unidas Podemos. Between 2018 and 2024, Spain faced an [institutional crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2018%E2%80%932023_Spanish_institutional_crisis "2018–2023 Spanish institutional crisis") surrounding the mandate of the [General Council of the Judiciary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Council_of_the_Judiciary "General Council of the Judiciary") (CGPJ), until finally the mandate got renovated.[[94]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-105) In January 2020, the [COVID-19](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") virus was confirmed to have [spread to Spain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Spain "COVID-19 pandemic in Spain"), causing life expectancy to drop by more than a year.[[95]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-106) The European Commission economic recovery package [Next Generation EU](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Next_Generation_EU "Next Generation EU") was created to support the EU member states to recover from the COVID-19 pandemic, and will be in use in the period 2021–2026. In March 2021, Spain became the sixth nation in the world to make [active euthanasia legal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legality_of_euthanasia "Legality of euthanasia").[[96]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-107)

Following the [general election on 23 July 2023](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2023_Spanish_general_election "2023 Spanish general election"), Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez once again formed a coalition government, this time with [Sumar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sumar_(electoral_platform) "Sumar (electoral platform)") (successors of [Unidas Podemos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unidas_Podemos "Unidas Podemos")).[[97]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-108) In 2024, the first non-independentist Catalan regional president in over a decade, [Salvador Illa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salvador_Illa "Salvador Illa"), was elected, normalising the constitutional and institutional relations between the national and the regional administrations. According to latest polls,[[98]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-auto1-109) only 17.3% of Catalans feel themselves as "only Catalan". 46% of Catalans would answer "as Spanish as Catalan", while 21.8% "more Catalan than Spanish".[[98]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-auto1-109) Accordind to a 2024 poll of University of Barcelona, over 50% of Catalans would vote against independence, while less than 40% would vote in favour.[[99]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-110)

Geography
---------

[![Image 30: Topographic map of the Iberian Peninsula showing Spain, Portugal, France, and Morocco](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Spain_topo.jpg/250px-Spain_topo.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Spain_topo.jpg)

Topographic map of Spain (excluding Canary Islands)

At 505,992 km 2 (195,365 sq mi), Spain is the world's [fiftieth largest country](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_and_outlying_territories_by_area "List of countries and outlying territories by area") and [Europe's fourth largest country](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Area_and_population_of_European_countries "Area and population of European countries"). At 3,715 m (12,188 ft), Mount [Teide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Teide "Teide") ([Tenerife](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife")) is the highest mountain peak in Spain and is the third largest volcano in the world from its base. Spain is a [transcontinental country](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_transcontinental_countries "List of transcontinental countries"), having territory in both [Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [Africa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Africa "Africa").

Spain lies between latitudes [27°](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/27th_parallel_north "27th parallel north") and [44° N](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/44th_parallel_north "44th parallel north"), and longitudes [19° W](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/19th_meridian_west "19th meridian west") and [5° E](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/5th_meridian_east "5th meridian east").

On the west, Spain is bordered by [Portugal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"); on the south, it is bordered by [Gibraltar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") and [Morocco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), through its [exclaves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") in North Africa ([Ceuta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ceuta "Ceuta") and [Melilla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melilla "Melilla"), and the peninsula of [de Vélez de la Gomera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pe%C3%B1%C3%B3n_de_V%C3%A9lez_de_la_Gomera "Peñón de Vélez de la Gomera")). On the northeast, along the [Pyrenees](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pyrenees "Pyrenees") mountain range, it is bordered by [France](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/France "France") and [Andorra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andorra "Andorra"). Along the Pyrenees in [Girona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Province_of_Girona "Province of Girona"), a small exclave town called [Llívia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ll%C3%ADvia "Llívia") is surrounded by France.

Extending to 1,214 km (754 mi), the [Portugal–Spain border](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portugal%E2%80%93Spain_border "Portugal–Spain border") is the [longest uninterrupted border](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_by_land_borders "List of countries and territories by land borders") within the [European Union](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Union "European Union").[[100]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-111)

### Islands

[![Image 31: Seashore of Mallorca island in Spain with turquoise water and many people enjoying the beach in summer](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/View_of_the_bay_at_Calo_des_Moro%2C_Mallorca_%28Spain%29_%2823899506181%29.jpg/250px-View_of_the_bay_at_Calo_des_Moro%2C_Mallorca_%28Spain%29_%2823899506181%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:View_of_the_bay_at_Calo_des_Moro,_Mallorca_(Spain)_(23899506181).jpg)

Caló d'es Moro, [Mallorca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mallorca "Mallorca") island

Spain also includes the [Balearic Islands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balearic_Islands "Balearic Islands") in the [Mediterranean Sea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), the [Canary Islands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands") in the Atlantic Ocean and a number of uninhabited islands on the Mediterranean side of the [Strait of Gibraltar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strait_of_Gibraltar "Strait of Gibraltar"), known as _[plazas de soberanía](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plazas\_de\_soberan%C3%ADa "Plazas de soberanía")_ ("places of sovereignty", or territories under Spanish sovereignty), such as the [Chafarinas Islands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chafarinas_Islands "Chafarinas Islands") and [Alhucemas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pe%C3%B1%C3%B3n_de_Alhucemas "Peñón de Alhucemas"). The peninsula of [de Vélez de la Gomera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pe%C3%B1%C3%B3n_de_V%C3%A9lez_de_la_Gomera "Peñón de Vélez de la Gomera") is also regarded as a _plaza de soberanía_. The isle of [Alborán](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alboran_Island "Alboran Island"), located in the Mediterranean between Spain and North Africa, is also administered by Spain, specifically by the municipality of [Almería](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almer%C3%ADa "Almería"), Andalusia. The little [Pheasant Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pheasant_Island "Pheasant Island") in the River [Bidasoa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bidasoa "Bidasoa") is a Spanish-French [condominium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Condominium_(international_law) "Condominium (international law)").

There are 11 major islands in Spain, all of them having their own governing bodies ([Cabildos insulares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cabildo_insular "Cabildo insular") in the Canaries, [Consells insulars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balearic_Islands#Administration "Balearic Islands") in Baleares). These islands are specifically mentioned by the Spanish Constitution, when fixing its Senatorial representation (Ibiza and Formentera are grouped, as they together form the [Pityusic islands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pityusic_Islands "Pityusic Islands"), part of the Balearic archipelago). These islands include [Tenerife](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife"), [Gran Canaria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria"), [Lanzarote](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lanzarote "Lanzarote"), [Fuerteventura](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fuerteventura "Fuerteventura"), [La Palma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/La_Palma "La Palma"), [La Gomera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/La_Gomera "La Gomera") and [El Hierro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/El_Hierro "El Hierro") in the Canarian archipelago and [Mallorca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mallorca "Mallorca"), [Ibiza](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ibiza "Ibiza"), [Menorca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Menorca "Menorca") and [Formentera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formentera "Formentera") in the Balearic archipelago.

### Mountains and rivers

[![Image 32: The Roque Cinchado rock formation is set against the backdrop of Mount Teide, with the volcanic landscape of Tenerife, Canary Islands, in clear, sunny day](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Roque_Cinchado_und_Teide.jpg/250px-Roque_Cinchado_und_Teide.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Roque_Cinchado_und_Teide.jpg)

[Teide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Teide "Teide"), still an [active volcano](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Active_volcano "Active volcano") in [Tenerife](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife"), Canary Islands, is the tallest peak in Spain.

Mainland Spain is a rather [mountainous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mountainous "Mountainous") landmass, dominated by high [plateaus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") and mountain chains. After the Pyrenees, the main mountain ranges are the [Cordillera Cantábrica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantabrian_Mountains "Cantabrian Mountains") (Cantabrian Range), [Sistema Ibérico](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sistema_Ib%C3%A9rico "Sistema Ibérico") (Iberian System), [Sistema Central](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sistema_Central "Sistema Central") (Central System), [Montes de Toledo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Montes_de_Toledo "Montes de Toledo"), [Sierra Morena](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sierra_Morena "Sierra Morena") and the [Sistema Bético](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sistema_B%C3%A9tico "Sistema Bético") (Baetic System) whose highest peak, the 3,478-metre-high (11,411-foot) [Mulhacén](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mulhac%C3%A9n "Mulhacén"), located in [Sierra Nevada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sierra_Nevada_(Spain) "Sierra Nevada (Spain)"), is the highest elevation in the Iberian Peninsula. The highest point in Spain is the [Teide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Teide "Teide"), a 3,718-metre (12,198 ft) active [volcano](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volcano "Volcano") in the Canary Islands. The [Meseta Central](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meseta_Central "Meseta Central") (often translated as 'Inner Plateau') is a vast plateau in the heart of peninsular Spain split in two by the Sistema Central.

There are several major [rivers in Spain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rivers_in_Spain "Rivers in Spain") such as the [Tagus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tagus "Tagus") (_Tajo_), [Ebro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ebro "Ebro"), [Guadiana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guadiana_River "Guadiana River"), [Douro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Douro "Douro") (_Duero_), [Guadalquivir](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guadalquivir "Guadalquivir"), [Júcar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J%C3%BAcar_River "Júcar River"), [Segura](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Segura_(river) "Segura (river)"), [Turia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turia_River "Turia River") and [Minho](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minho_River "Minho River") (_Miño_). [Alluvial plains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alluvial_plain "Alluvial plain") are found along the coast, the largest of which is that of the Guadalquivir in [Andalusia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andalusia "Andalusia").

### Climate

[![Image 33: Map of Spain presents its Köppen climate types, with tundra in gray, subarctic in dark teal, warm-summer humid continental in dark purple, dry-summer subarctic in light blue, warm-summer Mediterranean continental in green, subpolar oceanic in light green, oceanic in light green, humid subtropical in light yellow, warm-summer Mediterranean in yellow, hot-summer Mediterranean in orange, and hot semi-arid in dark orange](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Spain_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Spain_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Spain_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[Köppen climate classification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map of Spain

[![Image 34: Sharp-peaked mountains rise above a valley and trees under a clear sky](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Urriellu_desde_el_Pozo_de_La_Oracion.jpg/250px-Urriellu_desde_el_Pozo_de_La_Oracion.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Urriellu_desde_el_Pozo_de_La_Oracion.jpg)

Urriellu peak ([Naranjo de Bulnes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naranjo_de_Bulnes "Naranjo de Bulnes")) from Pozo de La Oracion, [Picos de Europa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Picos_de_Europa "Picos de Europa")

Three main climatic zones can be separated, according to geographical situation and [orographic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orography "Orography") conditions:[[101]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain#cite_note-112)

*   The [Mediterranean climate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") is characterised by warm/hot and dry summers and is the predominant climate in the country. It has two varieties: _Csa_ and _Csb_ according to the [Köppen climate classification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"). 
    *   The _Csa_ zone is associated with areas with hot summers. It is predominant in the Southern Mediterranean (except southeastern) and Southern Atlantic coast and inland throughout [Andalusia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andalusia "Andalusia"), Extremadura and much of the centre of the country. Some areas of Csa, mainly those inland, such as some areas of [Cast

Step-by-step plan:
1) Capture exact wording from a second authoritative source (CIA World Factbook) that states Spain’s continental/regional location to corroborate Britannica and add a government source.
2) Then collect a standards-based regional classification (UN M49/geoscheme) to document Spain’s placement within Europe/Southern Europe.
3) Compile a transparent report of candidate answers with quotes, links, dates, and notes on nuances (e.g., Spain’s North African territories, EU vs. Europe distinction), plus any uncertainties or conflicting interpretations found.

What we know so far:
- Britannica: “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe,” and it lists African territories (Canary Islands, Ceuta, Melilla).
- Wikipedia: “Spain … is a country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa,” also calls Spain “transcontinental.”

What’s missing/uncertain:
- Precise CIA World Factbook wording confirming European location/region (e.g., “Location: Southwestern Europe”) and any reference to extra-European territories.

Why this tool now:
- Scraping the CIA World Factbook page will provide verbatim text and page context from a reputable U.S. government source to strengthen evidence.

Proceeding to retrieve the CIA World Factbook page for Spain.

content.text:
Title: Spain - The World Factbook

URL Source: https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/countries/spain/

Markdown Content:
![Image 1: Building of the Government Subdelagation along the Gran Via in Bilbao, Bizkaia Province, Spain.](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/static/a506513f090ef428228d279f8a84d676/be79d/20190424_130902.jpg)

![Image 2: The Flag of Spain, with the Spanish Coat of Arms on the Yellow Band in the middle](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/static/d21e89432a1c16c63e5b3c2ecd020800/77260/SP-flag.jpg)

Country Flag

![Image 3: Map of Spain and Portugal showing major cities, bodies of water, and the Canary Islands](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/static/92aa0c63c04a33ec6f65bbf189303653/00757/SP-map.jpg)

Country Map

Special Country Products

![Image 4: Europe map with Spain in orange](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/static/47d3e90241a19a6fa481d08232888dcd/5da0f/SP-locator-map.jpg)

Locator Map

Introduction
------------

### [Background](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/background/)

Spain's powerful world empire of the 16th and 17th centuries ultimately yielded command of the seas to England. Spain remained neutral during both World Wars but suffered through a devastating civil war (1936-39) resulting in a dictatorship. A peaceful transition to democracy after the death of dictator Francisco FRANCO in 1975 and rapid economic modernization after Spain joined the EU in 1986 gave Spain a dynamic and rapidly growing economy. After a severe recession in the wake of the global financial crisis in 2008, Spain has posted solid years of GDP growth above the EU average. Unemployment has fallen but remains high, especially among youth. Spain is the euro-zone's fourth-largest economy. The country has faced increased domestic turmoil in recent years due to the independence movement in its restive Catalonia region.

Government
----------

### [Country name](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/country-name/)

**conventional long form:** Kingdom of Spain

**conventional short form:** Spain

**local long form:** Reino de España

**local short form:** España

**etymology:** derivation of the name España is uncertain; the Basque words _ezpain_ or _espan_("edge," as in a river bank) are possible sources, or the Punic word _span_, meaning "rabbit;" some academics tie it to the god Hesperus from Greco-Roman mythology

### [Capital](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/capital/)

**name:** Madrid

**geographic coordinates:** 40 24 N, 3 41 W

**time difference:** UTC+1 (6 hours ahead of Washington, DC, during Standard Time)

**daylight saving time:** +1hr, begins last Sunday in March; ends last Sunday in October

**time zone note:** Spain has two time zones, including the Canary Islands (UTC 0)

**etymology:** the meaning and origin of the name is unclear; the city grew from a small Moorish fort that was called Majerit in the first recorded mention in A.D. 932; some trace the modern-day name back to the Roman era, with the Latin word _materia_(materials) as a possible source

### [Administrative divisions](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/administrative-divisions/)

17 autonomous communities (_comunidades autonomas_, singular - _comunidad autonoma_) and 2 autonomous cities* (_ciudades autonomas_, singular - _ciudad autonoma_); Andalucia; Aragon; Asturias; Canarias (Canary Islands); Cantabria; Castilla-La Mancha; Castilla-Leon; Cataluña (Castilian), Catalunya (Catalan), Catalonha (Aranese) [Catalonia]; Ceuta*; Comunidad Valenciana (Castilian), Comunitat Valenciana (Valencian) [Valencian Community]; Extremadura; Galicia; Illes Baleares (Balearic Islands); La Rioja; Madrid; Melilla*; Murcia; Navarra (Castilian), Nafarroa (Basque) [Navarre]; Pais Vasco (Castilian), Euskadi (Basque) [Basque Country]

**note:** Spain administers the autonomous cities of Ceuta and Melilla and the three small islands of Islas Chafarinas, Peñón de Alhucemas, and Peñón de Velez de la Gomera, which are all located along the coast of Morocco; they are collectively referred to as Places of Sovereignty (Plazas de Soberania)

### [Constitution](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/constitution/)

**history:** several previous; latest approved by the General Courts 31 October 1978, passed by referendum 6 December 1978, signed by the king 27 December 1978, effective 29 December 1978

**amendment process:** proposed by the government, by the General Courts (the Congress or the Senate), or by the self-governing communities submitted through the government; passage requires three-fifths majority vote by both houses and passage by referendum if requested by one tenth of the members of either house; proposals disapproved by both houses are submitted to a joint committee, which submits an agreed upon text for another vote; passage requires two-thirds majority vote in Congress and simple majority vote in the Senate

### [Citizenship](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/citizenship/)

**citizenship by birth:** no

**citizenship by descent only:** at least one parent must be a citizen of Spain

**dual citizenship recognized:** only with select Latin American countries

**residency requirement for naturalization:** 10 years for persons with no ties to Spain

### [Executive branch](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/executive-branch/)

**chief of state:** King FELIPE VI (since 19 June 2014)

**head of government:** President of the Government of Spain (prime minister-equivalent) Pedro SANCHEZ PEREZ-CASTEJON (since 2 June 2018)

**cabinet:** Council of Ministers designated by the president

**election/appointment process:** the monarchy is hereditary; following legislative elections, the monarch usually proposes as president the leader of the majority party or coalition, who is then indirectly elected by the Congress of Deputies; vice president and Council of Ministers appointed by the president

**most recent election date:** 23 July 2023

**election results:** Congress of Deputies vote - 179 to 171 (16 November 2023)

**expected date of next election:** 31 July 2027

**note:** there is also a Council of State that is the supreme consultative organ of the government, but its recommendations are non-binding

### [Legislative branch](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/legislative-branch/)

**legislature name:** The Cortes (Las Cortes Generales)

**legislative structure:** bicameral

### [Judicial branch](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/judicial-branch/)

**highest court(s):** Supreme Court or Tribunal Supremo (consists of the court president and organized into the Civil Room, with a president and 9 judges; the Penal Room, with a president and 14 judges; the Administrative Room, with a president and 32 judges; the Social Room, with a president and 12 judges; and the Military Room, with a president and 7 judges); Constitutional Court or Tribunal Constitucional de Espana (consists of 12 judges)

**judge selection and term of office:** Supreme Court judges appointed by the monarch from candidates proposed by the General Council of the Judiciary Power, a 20-member governing board chaired by the monarch; judges can serve until age 70; Constitutional Court judges nominated by the National Assembly, executive branch, and the General Council of the Judiciary, and appointed by the monarch for 9-year terms

**subordinate courts:** National High Court; High Courts of Justice (in each of the autonomous communities); provincial courts; courts of first instance

### [Independence](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/independence/)

1492

**note:** the Iberian peninsula was home to a variety of independent kingdoms prior to the Muslim occupation that began in the early 8th century A.D. and lasted nearly seven centuries; the small Christian redoubts of the north began the reconquest almost immediately, culminating in the seizure of Granada in 1492; this completed the unification of several kingdoms and is traditionally considered the forging of present-day Spain

### [National holiday](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/national-holiday/)

National Day (Hispanic Day), 12 October (1492)

**note:** commemorates the arrival of explorer Christopher COLUMBUS in the Americas

### [Flag](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/flag/)

**description:** three horizontal bands of red (top), yellow (double-width), and red, with the national coat of arms on the left side of the yellow band; the coat of arms shows the emblems of the area's former kingdoms (clockwise from upper left: Castile, Leon, Navarre, and Aragon), which also used red and yellow as their colors; the stylized pomegranate at the bottom of the shield represents Granada; the two columns represent the Pillars of Hercules, which are promontories (Gibraltar and Ceuta) on the Strait of Gibraltar; a red scroll bears the imperial motto of "Plus Ultra" (further beyond), referring to Spanish lands outside Europe

### [National anthem(s)](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/national-anthems/)

**title:** "Himno Nacional Espanol" (National Anthem of Spain)

**lyrics/music:** no lyrics/unknown

**history:** adopted 1942;officially in use between 1770 and 1931, restored in 1939; the Spanish anthem was the first to be officially adopted; it first appeared in a 1761 military bugle-call book and was replaced by "Himno de Riego" in the years between 1931 and 1939; the long version of the anthem is used for the king, and the short version is used for the prince, prime minister, and occasions such as sporting events

Energy
------

### [Electricity generation sources](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/electricity-generation-sources/)

**fossil fuels:** 28% of total installed capacity (2023 est.)

### [Nuclear energy](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/nuclear-energy/)

**Number of operational nuclear reactors:** 7 (2025)

--------------

### [Broadcast media](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/broadcast-media/)

mix of publicly operated and privately owned TV and radio stations; hundreds of TV channels available, including national, regional, local, public, and international channels; satellite and cable TV available; multiple national radio networks, large number of regional radio networks, and larger number of local radio stations (2019)

--------------

comparison ranking: [total 42](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/merchant-marine/country-comparison/)

### [Ports](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/ports/)

**total ports:** 52 (2024)

**large:** 3
