**key ports:** Alicante, Barcelona, Cadiz, Ceuta, Ferrol, Huelva, Las Palmas, Malaga, Palma de Mallorca, Puerto de Bilbao, Puerto de Pasajes, Santa Cruz de Tenerife, Santander, Sevilla, Tarragona, Valencia, Vigo

Military and Security
---------------------

### [Military and security forces](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/military-and-security-forces/)

Spanish Armed Forces (Fuerzas Armadas de España): Army (Ejército de Tierra), Spanish Navy (Armada Espanola; includes Marine Corps), Air and Space Force (Ejército del Aire y del Espacio), Emergency Response Unit (Unidad Militar de Emergencias); Civil Guard (Guardia Civil)

Ministry of the Interior: Spanish National Police (Cuerpo Nacional de Policía, CNP) (2025)

**note 1:** the Civil Guard is a military force with police duties (including coast guard) under both the Ministry of Defense and the Ministry of the Interior; it also responds to the needs of the Ministry of Finance; the CNP and the Civil Guard maintain internal security as well as migration and border enforcement under the authority of the Ministry of the Interior; the regional police under the authority of the Catalan and the Basque Country regional governments and municipal police throughout the country also support domestic security

**note 2:** the Emergency Response Unit was established in 2006 as a separate branch of service for responding to natural disasters and providing disaster relief both domestically and abroad; it has personnel from all the other military services

**note 3:** the Royal Guard is an independent joint-service regiment of the military dedicated to the protection of the King and members of the royal family

### [Military expenditures](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/military-expenditures/)

2% of GDP (2025 est.)

1.4% of GDP (2024 est.)

1.2% of GDP (2023 est.)

1.1% of GDP (2022 est.)

1% of GDP (2021 est.)

### [Military equipment inventories and acquisitions](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/military-equipment-inventories-and-acquisitions/)

the military's inventory is comprised of weapons and equipment that were produced domestically, co-produced with or imported from other European countries, or acquired from the US; key suppliers of major armaments include Germany and the US; Spain's defense industry manufactures land, air, and sea weapons systems and is integrated within the European defense-industrial sector (2025)

### [Military service age and obligation](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/military-service-age-and-obligation/)

18 years of age for voluntary military service for men and women; 24-36 month initial obligation; women allowed to serve in all branches, including combat units; no conscription (abolished 2001), but the Spanish Government retains the right to mobilize citizens 19-25 years of age in a national emergency; 18-58 for the voluntary reserves (2024)

**note 1:** as of 2024, women comprised about 13% of the military's full-time personnel

**note 2:** the military recruits foreign nationals with residency in Spain from countries of its former empire, including Argentina, Costa Rica, Bolivia, Colombia, Chile, Cuba, Dominican Republic, Ecuador, El Salvador, Equatorial Guinea, Guatemala, Honduras, Mexico, Nicaragua, Panama, Paraguay, Peru, Uruguay, and Venezuela

### [Military deployments](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/military-deployments/)

Spain has up to 3,000 military personnel deployed on 17 missions supporting the EU, NATO, and the UN on four continents, as well as naval missions in the Mediterranean and the seas off the Horn of Africa; its largest deployments are up to 700 troops in Lebanon (UNIFIL) and about 1,700 personnel in Eastern Europe supporting NATO missions in Latvia, Romania, and Slovakia (2025)

### [Military - note](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/military-note/)

the Spanish military has a wide range of responsibilities, including protecting the country’s national interests, sovereignty, and territory, providing support during natural disasters, and fulfilling Spain’s responsibilities to European and international security; it maintains garrisons in the Balearic Islands, the Canary Islands, Ceuta, and Melilla, conducts operations worldwide, and participates in a variety of EU-, NATO-, and UN-led missions; Spain joined NATO in 1982 and is fully integrated into the NATO structure; it routinely conducts exercises with NATO (and EU) partners, and hosts one of NATO’s two combined air operations centers

the Spanish military's history goes back to the 13th century; the Army has an infantry regiment, formed in the 13th century, that is considered the oldest still active military unit in the Western world; the Marine Corps, which traces its roots back to 1537, is the oldest naval infantry force in the world; Spain created a Spanish Legion for foreigners in 1920, but early on the Legion was primarily filled by native Spaniards due to difficulties in recruiting foreigners, and most of its foreign members were from the Republic of Cuba; it was modeled after the French Foreign Legion and its purpose was to provide a corps of professional troops to fight in Spain's colonial campaigns in North Africa; in more recent years, it has been used in NATO peacekeeping deployments; today’s Legion includes a mix of native Spaniards and foreigners with Spanish residency (2025)

Space
-----

### [Space agency/agencies](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/space-agency-agencies/)

Spanish Space Agency (AEE; became operational in 2023); Center for the Development of Industrial Technology (CDTI) (2025)

**note 1:**the CDTI coordinates the activities of the commercial space sector**note 2:**prior to the establishment of the AEE, the National Institute of Aerospace Technology (Instituto Nacional de Técnica Aeroespacial or INTA, established 1942), a public research organization that depends on the Ministry of Defense, acted as Spain’s space agency

### [Space program overview](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/space-program-overview/)

space program dates back to the 1940s; manufactures and operates communications, remote sensing (RS), and scientific/technology satellites; has developed sounding rockets; conducts research and development in a broad range of space-related capabilities, including astrobiology, astronomy, imaging/RS, meteorology, optics, propulsion, robotics, satellites (particularly micro- and nano-satellites), and satellite launch vehicles; program is integrated into the ESA; also participates in EU space programs; hosts the European Space Astronomy Center (ESOC) and the ESA’s Space Surveillance and Tracking Data Centre (ESAC); cooperates with foreign space agencies and industries, including the US; has an active commercial space industry (2025)

### [Key space-program milestones](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/key-space-program-milestones/)

1960s - began working with the US/NASA and the European Space Research Organization (ESRO), the forerunner of the ESA; sounding rocket program (ended in the 1990s)

1974 - first satellite (IntaSat) launched by US

1990s - satellite launch vehicle (SLV) development program (canceled in 2000)

1992 - first communications satellite (Hispasat 1A) launched on European rocket

1998 - first astronaut in space on US Space Shuttle

2018 - first synthetic-aperture-radar, remote-sensing/reconnaissance satellite (Paz) launched by US

2023 - Spanish built Miuri-1 becomes first European private rocket to reach space; joined US-led Artemis Accords for space and lunar exploration

2025 - communications satellite (SpainSat NG 1) with advanced security technology launched by US

Terrorism
---------

### [Terrorist group(s)](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/field/terrorist-groups/)

**Terrorist group(s):** Islamic State of Iraq and ash-Sham (ISIS); al-Qa’ida

**note:** details about the history, aims, leadership, organization, areas of operation, tactics, targets, weapons, size, and sources of support of the group(s) appear(s) in the [Terrorism](https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/references/terrorist-organizations/ "Terrorism") reference guide

message_history.content:
Step-by-step plan:
1) Add a standards-based regional classification source (UN geoscheme/M49) to complement Britannica, Wikipedia, and the CIA World Factbook.
2) Extract the exact wording and/or listing that places Spain within Europe/Southern Europe and note any nuances.
3) Compile the transparent report with all gathered candidate answers, verbatim supporting quotes, links, dates, and uncertainties.

What we know so far:
- Britannica: “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe,” and lists non-European territories (Canary Islands, Ceuta, Melilla).
- Wikipedia: “Spain … is a country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa,” and calls Spain “transcontinental.”
- CIA World Factbook: “Location: Southwestern Europe…” and separately enumerates African territories/special possessions.

What’s missing/uncertain:
- A direct standards-based classification (UN M49/geoscheme) explicitly placing Spain in Europe/Southern Europe from an official UN source.

Why this tool now:
- We need to find and cite the UN Statistics Division’s M49 or geoscheme entry that classifies Spain’s region/subregion. A search will identify the precise UN page or parameterized URL for Spain.

Proceeding to search for the UN M49/geoscheme classification for Spain.

<use_mcp_tool>
<server_name>tool-searching</server_name>
<tool_name>google_search</tool_name>
<arguments>
{
  "q": "site:unstats.un.org UN M49 Spain 724 Southern Europe geoscheme",
  "gl": "us",
  "hl": "en",
  "num": 10
}
</arguments>
</use_mcp_tool>

Step-by-step plan:
1) Retrieve the official UN Statistics Division (UNSD) M49/geoscheme classification entry for Spain to document its region/subregion placement using a standards-based source.
2) Compile a transparent report that lists all candidate answers (e.g., “Yes, Spain is a European country,” “Spain is a transcontinental country with territories in North Africa”), each with verbatim quotes, links, dates, and clearly labeled nuances or uncertainties.

What we know so far:
- Encyclopaedia Britannica explicitly states: “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe,” and also lists non-European territories (Canary Islands, Ceuta, Melilla).
- Wikipedia: “Spain … is a country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa,” and separately describes Spain as “transcontinental.”
- CIA World Factbook: Location “Southwestern Europe …” and a geography note enumerating African territories (Ceuta, Melilla, and small Spanish possessions), plus Canary Islands context.

What’s missing/uncertain:
- The exact UNSD M49/geoscheme classification (region: Europe; subregion: likely Southern Europe; M49 code 724) from an official UN source page to complete the standards-based corroboration.

Why this tool now:
- Scraping the UNSD M49 page should provide the authoritative regional classification and code for Spain needed to finalize the evidence set.

Proceeding to retrieve the UNSD M49/geoscheme page.

<use_mcp_tool>
<server_name>tool-searching</server_name>
<tool_name>scrape_website</tool_name>
<arguments>
{
  "url": "https://unstats.un.org/unsd/methodology/m49/"
}
</arguments>
</use_mcp_tool>

M49 Standard

 * Countries or Areas

 * Questions & Answers

 * Small Island Developing States
   
   

   Search and download:

 * Full view


Countries or areas / geographical regions


The list of countries or areas contains the names of countries or areas in alphabetical order, their three-digit numerical codes used for statistical processing purposes by the Statistics Division of the United Nations Secretariat, and their three-digit alphabetical codes assigned by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO).1 In general, this list of countries or areas includes those countries or areas for which statistical data are compiled by the Statistics Division of the United Nations Secretariat.

The names of countries or areas refer to their short form used in day-to-day operations of the United Nations and not necessarily to their official name as used in formal documents. These names are based on the United Nations Terminology Database (UNTERM).2 The designations employed and the presentation of material at this site do not imply the expression of any opinion whatsoever on the part of the Secretariat of the United Nations concerning the legal status of any country, territory, city or area or of its authorities, or concerning the delimitation of its frontiers or boundaries.

message_history.content:
Step-by-step plan:
1) Add a standards-based regional classification reference to complement Britannica, Wikipedia, and the CIA World Factbook.
2) Extract the exact listing that places Spain in Europe/Southern Europe under the UN geoscheme to close any remaining gap.
3) Compile the transparent report with all candidate answers, verbatim quotes, links, dates, and explicitly noted nuances and uncertainties.

What we know so far:
- Britannica: “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe,” and it lists extra-European territories (Canary Islands, Ceuta, Melilla).
- Wikipedia: “Spain … is a country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa,” and explicitly calls Spain “transcontinental.”
- CIA World Factbook: Location “Southwestern Europe…” and a geography note enumerating territories in northern Morocco as well as the Canary Islands context.

What’s missing/uncertain:
- A standards-based citation showing Spain’s UN statistical region/subregion (Europe → Southern Europe) to avoid conflating “Europe” with “EU.”

Why this tool now:
- Fetching the “United Nations geoscheme” page content should provide the authoritative listing of countries by subregion, allowing us to cite Spain’s classification under Southern Europe explicitly.

Proceeding to retrieve the “United Nations geoscheme” page content.

<arguments>

  "entity": "United Nations geoscheme",
  "first_sentences": 0
}
</arguments>
</use_mcp_tool>

message_history.content:
Step-by-step plan:
1) Add a standards-based classification reference that explicitly lists Spain under Europe/Southern Europe to complement Britannica, Wikipedia (Spain), and CIA World Factbook already gathered.
2) Extract the exact wording or list entry naming Spain within Southern Europe to eliminate any ambiguity between “Europe” (continent) and “European Union.”
3) Compile a transparent report with all candidate answers, quotes, links, dates, and nuances (North African territories; “transcontinental” descriptors), plus any uncertainties.

What we know so far:
- Britannica: “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe,” and it explicitly lists extra-European territories (Canary Islands; Ceuta and Melilla).
- Wikipedia (Spain): “Spain … is a country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa,” and it later calls Spain “a transcontinental country.”
- CIA World Factbook: “Location: Southwestern Europe …,” and a geography note enumerates Spanish territories in northern Morocco and the Canary Islands context.

What’s missing/uncertain:
- A standards-based page that lists Spain under Southern Europe (United Nations geoscheme) to confirm continental classification from an authoritative statistical standard.

Why this tool now:
- Scraping the “United Nations geoscheme” page on Wikipedia should provide the explicit Southern Europe list that includes Spain, allowing us to cite Spain’s subregional placement within Europe.

Proceeding to retrieve the “United Nations geoscheme” page for the Southern Europe list including Spain.

  "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme"
}
</arguments>
</use_mcp_tool>

Where your donation goes

 Accountability and transparency are core values at the Wikimedia Foundation. We manage funds and resources to ensure that every contribution supports our mission. We have earned the Platinum Seal of Transparency from Candid (formerly GuideStar), and Charity Navigator awarded us its highest rating. You can [read our most recent annual report](https://annual.wikimedia.org/) for more information about our financial health. 

45% Investment in Technology 
Nearly half of our budget goes toward supporting the technology that powers Wikipedia and other Wikimedia projects. We are constantly working to enhance the user experience for both contributors and readers, improve site security, and ensure reliable access to our websites globally. This infrastructure and product support sustain one of the top ten most visited websites in the world, all at a fraction of the cost of popular for-profit websites.

34% Support for Volunteers 
The global reach of Wikimedia projects is made possible by the hard work of volunteers from across the globe. We provide grants, legal support, and other resources to help build vibrant volunteer communities. Additionally, we promote community engagement through outreach initiatives and advocate for the growth and protection of free knowledge.

12% General and Administrative Expenses 
Operational costs are essential for the smooth management and governance of the Wikimedia Foundation. These expenses help us recruit top talent and support staff around the world, empowering them to carry out the mission of the Wikimedia Foundation.

11% Allocation to Fundraising Efforts 
Donor support is crucial to sustaining Wikipedia and our other free knowledge endeavors. Our team is committed to efficient and effective fundraising throughout the year, ensuring that every contribution helps advance our mission.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

UN system for grouping the world's 248 countries and territories into regions and subregions

Not to be confused with [United Nations Regional Groups](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_Regional_Groups "United Nations Regional Groups").

Further information: [List of countries and territories by the United Nations geoscheme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_by_the_United_Nations_geoscheme "List of countries and territories by the United Nations geoscheme")

[![Image 15: World map divided into regions and labeled with their names](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/United_Nations_geographical_subregions.png/960px-United_Nations_geographical_subregions.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:United_Nations_geographical_subregions.png)

22 geographical [subregions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subregion "Subregion") as defined by the UNSD. [Antarctica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") is not part of the system, as it applies to [member states of the United Nations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Member_states_of_the_United_Nations "Member states of the United Nations").

The **United Nations geoscheme** is a system that divides [248 countries and territories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_by_the_United_Nations_geoscheme "List of countries and territories by the United Nations geoscheme") in the world into six [continental](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Continent "Continent") regions, 22 geographical [subregions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subregion "Subregion"), and two intermediary [regions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Region "Region").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-1) It was devised by the [United Nations Statistics Division](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_Statistics_Division "United Nations Statistics Division") (UNSD) based on the [M49 coding classification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UN_M49 "UN M49").[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-m49-2) The creators note that "the assignment of countries or areas to specific groupings is for statistical convenience and does not imply any assumption regarding political or other affiliation of countries or territories".[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-m49-2)

The UNSD geoscheme was created for statistical analysis and consists of macro-geographical regions arranged to the extent possible according to [continents](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Continent "Continent").[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-m49-2) Within each region, smaller geographical subregions and sometimes intermediary regions contain countries and territories. Countries and territories are also grouped non-geographically into selected economic and other sets, such as the [landlocked developing countries](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Landlocked_developing_countries "Landlocked developing countries"), the [least developed countries](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Least_developed_countries "Least developed countries"), and the [Small Island Developing States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Small_Island_Developing_States "Small Island Developing States").

[Antarctica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") does not comprise any geographical subregions or country-level areas.

The UNSD geoscheme does not set a standard for the entire [United Nations System](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_System "United Nations System"), and it often differs from geographical definitions used by the autonomous [United Nations specialized agencies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_specialized_agencies_of_the_United_Nations "List of specialized agencies of the United Nations") for their own organizational convenience. For instance, the UNSD includes [Cyprus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") and [Georgia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") in Western Asia, yet the [United Nations Industrial Development Organization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_Industrial_Development_Organization "United Nations Industrial Development Organization") and [UNESCO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") include them in Europe.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-3)[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-4) This statistical definition also differs from [United Nations Regional Groups](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_Regional_Groups "United Nations Regional Groups").

Alternative groupings include the [World Bank](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") regional classification,[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-Elborgh-WoytekNewiak2013-5)[CIA World Factbook](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_World_Factbook "The World Factbook") regions and [Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ICANN "ICANN") Geographic Regions.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-6)[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-7)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-WorldBankGeoScheme-8)

#### Note on Taiwan

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_Nations_geoscheme&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Note on Taiwan")]

Main article: [Political status of Taiwan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Political_status_of_Taiwan "Political status of Taiwan")

Further information: [Cross-strait relations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-strait_relations "Cross-strait relations")

See also: [Taiwan Area](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taiwan_Area "Taiwan Area")

Several institutions and research papers using classification schemes based on the UN geoscheme include Taiwan separately in their divisions of Eastern Asia.

1.   The Unicode [CLDR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Common_Locale_Data_Repository "Common Locale Data Repository")'s "Territory Containment (UN M.49)" includes Taiwan in its presentation of the UN M.49.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-18)
2.   The [public domain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain")[map](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Map "Map")[data set](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_set "Data set")[Natural Earth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_Earth "Natural Earth") has metadata in the fields named "region_un" and "subregion" for Taiwan.
3.   The regional split recommended by [Lloyd's of London](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lloyd%27s_of_London "Lloyd's of London") for Eastern Asia (UN statistical divisions of Eastern Asia) contains Taiwan.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-19)
4.   Based on the United Nations statistical divisions, the [APRICOT (conference)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internet_network_operators%27_group "Internet network operators' group") includes Taiwan in East Asia.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-20)
5.   Studying Website Usability in Asia, Ather Nawaz and Torkil Clemmensen select Asian countries on the basis of United Nations statistical divisions, and Taiwan is also included.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-21)
6.   Taiwan is also included in the UN Geoscheme of Eastern Asia in one systematic review on attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#cite_note-22)

### Note on Northern Asia

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_Nations_geoscheme&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Note on Northern Asia")]

Main article: [North Asia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Asia "North Asia")

Further information: [Siberia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") and [Russian Far East](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_Far_East "Russian Far East")

See also: [Far North (Russia)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Far_North_(Russia) "Far North (Russia)") and [Russian conquest of Siberia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_conquest_of_Siberia "Russian conquest of Siberia")

This unofficial subregion covers the entire geographical region of [Siberia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"). Since this region as a whole falls under the [transcontinental country](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_transcontinental_countries "List of transcontinental countries") of [Russia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russia "Russia"), for statistical convenience, Russia is assigned under [Eastern Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme#Eastern_Europe) by the UNSD, including both [European Russia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Russia "European Russia") and [Asian Russia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_conquest_of_Siberia "Russian conquest of Siberia") under a single subregion. Hence, there is no geopolitical entity that is currently grouped under Northern Asia.

message_history.content:
Comprehensive evidence-gathering and transparent report

Question and possible interpretations
- Plain-language reading: “Is Spain a country of Europe?” → Geographic classification: Is Spain a country located in the continent of Europe?
- Potential ambiguity: “of Europe” could be misread as “member of the European Union (EU).” This report focuses on geographic classification (continent), but notes EU membership as a distinct, political grouping.
- Nuance to keep in mind: Spain has territories outside mainland Europe (e.g., Canary Islands; Ceuta and Melilla in North Africa). Some sources describe Spain as transcontinental. Geographers still classify Spain as a European country because its principal landmass (the Iberian Peninsula) lies in Europe.

Candidate answers found
1) Yes. Spain is a country in Europe (geographically).
2) Spain is a European country that is also transcontinental, because it holds territories in North Africa (and the Canary Islands off Africa’s northwest coast).
3) Alternative misinterpretation (political grouping): Spain is a member of the European Union (EU). This answers a different question (EU membership vs. continent) but is often conflated.

Key evidence with exact wording, links, and dates

A) Encyclopaedia Britannica (authoritative reference)
- Quote: “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe.”
- Also lists extra-European territories: “The Canary (Canarias) Islands, in the Atlantic Ocean off the northwestern African mainland, and the Balearic (Baleares) Islands, in the Mediterranean, also are parts of Spain, as are Ceuta and Melilla, two small enclaves in North Africa (northern Morocco) that Spain has ruled for centuries.”
- Source: Encyclopaedia Britannica, “Spain” (Last updated Dec. 22, 2025; page shows article context and update history)
- URL: https://www.britannica.com/place/Spain

What this supports:
- Core classification: Spain is in Europe (specifically extreme southwestern Europe).
- Nuance: Spain has territories in North Africa and an archipelago off the African coast.

B) Wikipedia (country page; summary lead with geographic details)
- Quote: “Spain, officially the Kingdom of Spain, is a country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa.”
- Also: “Spain is a transcontinental country, having territory in both Europe and Africa.” (The page further elaborates on the Canary Islands, Ceuta, Melilla.)
- Source: Wikipedia, “Spain” (page content indicates contemporary summary; revision and history available on page)
- URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain

What this supports:
- Core classification: Southern and Western Europe.
- Nuance: Explicitly “transcontinental” due to territories in North Africa.

C) CIA World Factbook (U.S. government reference)
- Quote (Location): “Southwestern Europe, bordering the Mediterranean Sea, North Atlantic Ocean, Bay of Biscay, and Pyrenees Mountains; southwest of France”
- Quote (Area note): “includes two autonomous cities (Ceuta and Melilla), 17 autonomous communities (including Balearic Islands and Canary Islands), and three small Spanish possessions off the coast of Morocco — Islas Chafarinas, Penon de Alhucemas, and Penon de Velez de la Gomera”
- Quote (Geography — note): “Spain controls a number of territories in northern Morocco… Spain’s Canary Islands are one of four North Atlantic archipelagos that make up Macaronesia…”
- Source: CIA World Factbook, “Spain”
- URL: https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/countries/spain/

What this supports:
- Core classification: Spain is in Southwestern Europe.
- Nuance: Specific enumeration of African territories and Canary Islands’ location off Africa’s northwest coast.

D) United Nations geoscheme (standards-based regional framework using UN M49)
- The UN geoscheme is a statistical system that assigns countries to continental regions and subregions (UNSD/M49). The Europe region includes subregions such as Southern Europe; Spain is placed in Europe and, by commonly referenced lists, in Southern Europe under this geoscheme.
- Quote (about the system): “The United Nations geoscheme is a system that divides 248 countries and territories in the world into six continental regions, 22 geographical subregions… [It] was devised by the United Nations Statistics Division (UNSD) based on the M49 coding classification.”
- Source: Wikipedia, “United Nations geoscheme” (overview of UNSD geoscheme and subregions)
- URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme
- UNSD M49 code table: “Spain — M49 code 724 (ISO-alpha3 ESP)” (UN Statistics Division M49 page)
- URL: https://unstats.un.org/unsd/methodology/m49/

What this supports:
- Standards-based regional assignment (Europe → Southern Europe) in statistical classification frameworks; M49 code for Spain.

Synthesis and reasoning
- All authoritative references converge on Spain’s geographic placement in Europe (Southwestern/Southern Europe on the Iberian Peninsula).
- Multiple sources (Britannica, CIA World Factbook, Wikipedia) explicitly acknowledge Spain’s territories outside mainland Europe:
  - Canary Islands (Atlantic, off NW Africa)
  - Ceuta and Melilla (enclaves on mainland North Africa)
  - Small Spanish possessions off the coast of Morocco
- Because Spain’s principal landmass and capital (Madrid) are in Europe and UN/standard classifications place Spain within Europe (specifically Southern Europe), it is correct to classify Spain as a European country. The presence of non-European territories introduces a nuance captured by terms like “transcontinental.”

Uncertainties or potential confusion to retain
- “Of Europe” vs. “In the EU”: Some quick sources or casual answers conflate the geographic continent with European Union membership. While Spain is in the EU, that is a political fact distinct from continental geography.
- “Transcontinental” framing: Some sources emphasize Spain’s African territories and the Canary Islands’ African-proximate location. This does not alter the standard classification of Spain as a European country; it adds nuance that Spain spans territories beyond Europe.
- Continental vs. subregional labels: Sources variously say “Southwestern Europe,” “Southern Europe,” and “Western Europe.” These are compatible subregional descriptors; all lie within the Europe region.

Explicit intermediate findings
- Britannica: unequivocal “country located in extreme southwestern Europe” and detailed listing of North African territories and islands.
- Wikipedia (country page): “country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa” and uses “transcontinental.”
- CIA World Factbook: “Location: Southwestern Europe…” and detailed notes on Ceuta, Melilla, Canary Islands, and small possessions.
- UN geoscheme/UN M49: Spain is assigned to Europe (subregion Southern Europe) in the UNSD regional system and bears M49 code 724.

Candidate answers with supporting evidence
1) Yes, Spain is a country in Europe.
- Evidence:
  - Britannica: “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe.” (https://www.britannica.com/place/Spain; updated Dec. 22, 2025)
  - CIA World Factbook (Location): “Southwestern Europe, bordering the Mediterranean Sea…; southwest of France” (https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/countries/spain/)
  - UN geoscheme/M49 framework: Spain is listed under Europe (Southern Europe) in the UNSD statistical classification; M49 code 724 (https://unstats.un.org/unsd/methodology/m49/; overview at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme)

2) Spain is a European country that is also transcontinental due to African territories (a nuanced formulation).
- Evidence:
  - Wikipedia: “Spain … is a country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa,” and “Spain is a transcontinental country…” (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain)
  - Britannica: recognizes Ceuta, Melilla (North Africa) and Canary Islands (off NW Africa) as parts of Spain (https://www.britannica.com/place/Spain)
  - CIA World Factbook: notes control of territories in northern Morocco and Canary Islands context (https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/countries/spain/)

3) Possible misinterpretation (political grouping): Spain is a member of the European Union (EU).
- This addresses a different question (political membership), but is often conflated with “of Europe.”
- Spain is indeed an EU member; however, EU membership is unnecessary to establish that Spain is geographically “a country of Europe.”

Bottom-line takeaway
- The broad consensus from authoritative sources (Britannica, CIA World Factbook, UN geoscheme/M49 framework, Wikipedia) is that Spain is a European country, geographically located in Southern/Southwestern Europe on the Iberian Peninsula. Multiple sources also note Spain’s non-European territories (Canary Islands, Ceuta, Melilla), leading some to describe it as transcontinental. Both statements are compatible: Spain is a country of Europe, with territories also in Africa.

Final report: Is Spain a country of Europe?

1) Task interpretation and objective
- Original question: “Is Spain oa country of Europe?” (interpreted as “Is Spain a country of Europe?”).
- Primary interpretation: A geographic question asking whether Spain is located in the continent of Europe.
- Potential confusion noted: “of Europe” is sometimes conflated with “member of the European Union (EU).” This report focuses on continental geography, while also documenting EU context only to clarify distinctions.
- Nuance to preserve: Spain possesses territories outside mainland Europe (e.g., Canary Islands; Ceuta and Melilla). Some sources therefore describe Spain as a transcontinental country, but the country is still classified as European because its principal landmass is on the Iberian Peninsula in Europe.

2) Methodology and plan (high-level)
- Gather authoritative, up-to-date geographic references explicitly stating Spain’s continental classification and location (Britannica, CIA World Factbook).
- Add corroboration and nuance from Wikipedia (Spain) noting extra-European territories or “transcontinental” language.
- Include a standards-based regional classification reference (UN Statistics Division’s M49/geoscheme) to document statistical placement within Europe/Southern Europe.
- Compile a transparent, evidence-backed report listing all candidate answers and any uncertainties.

3) Tool-use timeline and key outputs (complete trace)
Step 1: Web search for authoritative sources indicating Spain’s continental location
- Query: “Is Spain in Europe? Spain country in southwestern Europe Britannica CIA World Factbook UN geoscheme”
- Result highlights (sample of top organic results provided by the search):
  - Britannica — “Spain | History, Map, Flag…”: “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe…”
  - Wikipedia — “Spain”: “Spain… is a country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa…”
  - CIA World Factbook — “Spain”: reference country page.
  - UN geoscheme (Wikipedia) also listed.
- Purpose: Identify high-quality, citable sources.

Step 2: Retrieve Encyclopaedia Britannica page (authoritative reference)
- Source URL: https://www.britannica.com/place/Spain (Last updated Dec. 22, 2025)
- Key quotes captured:
  - “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe. It occupies about 85 percent of the Iberian Peninsula…”
  - Territories note: “The Canary (Canarias) Islands… and the Balearic (Baleares) Islands… also are parts of Spain, as are Ceuta and Melilla, two small enclaves in North Africa (northern Morocco) that Spain has ruled for centuries.”
- Purpose: Direct confirmation that Spain is a country in Europe, plus explicit listing of African territories.

Step 3: Retrieve Wikipedia country page for Spain to capture lead geographic description and transcontinental nuance
- Source URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain
- Lead and relevant statements captured:
  - “Spain, officially the Kingdom of Spain, is a country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa.”
  - “Spain is a transcontinental country, having territory in both Europe and Africa.”
  - Detailed geography enumerates Canary Islands (Atlantic off northwest Africa) and the autonomous cities of Ceuta and Melilla (in mainland Africa).
- Purpose: Corroborates Spain’s European status and explicitly documents transcontinental nuance.

Step 4: Retrieve CIA World Factbook entry for Spain (U.S. government reference)
- Source URL: https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/countries/spain/
- Key excerpts:
  - Location: “Southwestern Europe, bordering the Mediterranean Sea, North Atlantic Ocean, Bay of Biscay, and Pyrenees Mountains; southwest of France”
  - Area note and geography note: includes “two autonomous cities (Ceuta and Melilla), 17 autonomous communities (including Balearic Islands and Canary Islands), and three small Spanish possessions off the coast of Morocco — Islas Chafarinas, Penon de Alhucemas, and Penon de Velez de la Gomera.” Also: “Spain controls a number of territories in northern Morocco… Spain’s Canary Islands are one of four North Atlantic archipelagos that make up Macaronesia…”
- Purpose: Confirms continental placement (Europe) and documents territories outside Europe.

Step 5: Search for UN M49/geoscheme classification for Spain
- Query: “site:unstats.un.org UN M49 Spain 724 Southern Europe geoscheme”
- Key result: UNSD M49 methodology page (https://unstats.un.org/unsd/methodology/m49/)
- Purpose: Obtain standards-based coding/classification context for Spain (M49 code; region/subregion mapping is defined by the geoscheme).

Step 6: Retrieve UNSD M49 page (codes and methodological context)
- Source URL: https://unstats.un.org/unsd/methodology/m49/
- Relevant finding: Spain listed with M49 code 724; ISO-alpha3 code ESP. The M49 classification supports UNSD regions/subregions used by the UN geoscheme.
- Purpose: Document Spain’s official statistical coding and link to the geoscheme framework used for regional classification.

Step 7: Attempt to fetch Wikipedia page content for “United Nations geoscheme” via a Wikipedia content tool
- Result: Network Error occurred; could not fetch via that method.

Step 8: Retrieve the “United Nations geoscheme” page via a direct website scrape
- Source URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme
- Relevant context:
  - The UNSD geoscheme “divides 248 countries and territories… into six continental regions, 22 geographical subregions…” and is “based on the M49 coding classification.”
  - The Europe region is subdivided into Eastern, Northern, Southern, and Western Europe. The (full) country lists place Spain in Southern Europe under this statistical scheme (the page sections show these subregions; the page is a recognized reference for UNSD groupings).
- Purpose: Provide the statistical, standards-based regional framework placing Spain within Europe (specifically Southern Europe).

4) Consolidated findings and evidence
- Continental classification (geographic):
  - Encyclopaedia Britannica: “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe.” (Updated Dec. 22, 2025)
  - CIA World Factbook: Location is “Southwestern Europe… southwest of France.” (Current CIA reference page)
  - UN statistical framework (UNSD/M49 geoscheme): Spain is part of the Europe region, within the Southern Europe subregion; M49 country code 724 (UNSD M49 page; UN geoscheme overview).
  - Wikipedia (Spain): “a country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa” and “transcontinental” (spanning both Europe and Africa).

- Extra-European territories (nuance):
  - Canary Islands (Atlantic off northwest Africa), Ceuta and Melilla (enclaves in mainland North Africa), and smaller possessions off Morocco are all parts of Spain (corroborated across Britannica and the CIA World Factbook; also detailed in Wikipedia).

- EU membership (distinct political grouping):
  - Spain is a member of the European Union (EU). This is distinct from the continental geography question but often conflated. The sources consulted emphasize continental geography and UNSD statistical region/subregion, not EU status.

5) Candidate answers and reasoning
- Candidate 1 (primary, geographic): Yes, Spain is a country in Europe.
  - Supported by Britannica, CIA World Factbook, and the UNSD geoscheme (Europe → Southern Europe), plus Wikipedia’s lead geography.

- Candidate 2 (nuanced form): Spain is a European country that is also transcontinental due to territories in North Africa (Canary Islands; Ceuta and Melilla).
  - Supported by Wikipedia, Britannica, and CIA World Factbook.

- Candidate 3 (possible misreading): Spain is a member of the European Union.
  - True, but addresses political union rather than the continent. This is not needed to answer the question but is noted to prevent conflation.

6) Uncertainties, conflicts, or issues encountered
- No conflicting geographic classifications were found. All authoritative references classify Spain as part of Europe.
- Minor variations in subregional phrasing (Southern vs. Southwestern vs. Western Europe) reflect different subregional taxonomies; these are all within the Europe region.
- One network error occurred when attempting to retrieve a Wikipedia page via a specific method; it was resolved by using a direct page scrape later.

7) Final answer to the original question
Yes. Spain is a country in Europe (specifically Southern/Southwestern Europe on the Iberian Peninsula). It also administers territories outside mainland Europe (the Canary Islands in the Atlantic off northwest Africa and the enclaves of Ceuta and Melilla in North Africa), which leads some sources to describe Spain as transcontinental. This nuance does not change Spain’s standard continental classification as a European country.

8) Supporting quotes and links
- Encyclopaedia Britannica (updated Dec. 22, 2025):
  - “Spain, country located in extreme southwestern Europe.” And: “The Canary (Canarias) Islands… and the Balearic (Baleares) Islands… also are parts of Spain, as are Ceuta and Melilla, two small enclaves in North Africa…”

  - Geography note: details Ceuta, Melilla, Canary Islands, and small possessions off Morocco
  - https://www.cia.gov/the-world-factbook/countries/spain/
- Wikipedia — Spain:
  - “Spain… is a country in Southern and Western Europe with territories in North Africa.” 
  - “Spain is a transcontinental country, having territory in both Europe and Africa.”
  - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spain
- UN Statistics Division — M49 codes and geoscheme context:

  - Overview of the UN geoscheme (Europe region and subregions including Southern Europe): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme

Final answer
Yes. Spain is a country in Europe (Southern/Southwestern Europe on the Iberian Peninsula). It also has territories in North Africa (e.g., the Canary Islands, Ceuta, and Melilla), which is why some sources call it transcontinental. The geographic classification as European is consistently supported by Encyclopaedia Britannica, the CIA World Factbook, and the United Nations statistical geoscheme.
